proposals.title: Coherent Conditional Generation via Probabilistic Graphical Attribute Completion
proposals.motivation: Current conditional generative models (e.g., GANs, diffusion models) often fail when given partial, noisy, or contradictory attribute specifications, leading to implausible outputs. This is because they lack an explicit, structured model of the complex dependencies and causal relationships between attributes. A principled method to infer a coherent, complete set of conditioning signals from ambiguous user input is a critical unsolved problem for controllable generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a structured probabilistic graphical model (PGM) for attribute inference with a powerful conditional generative network will produce more globally consistent and semantically plausible outputs from incomplete or uncertain specifications than end-to-end neural approaches alone.
proposals.method: We will design a Bayesian network to model joint dependencies between semantic attributes (e.g., object categories, materials, spatial relations). Given partial input, we perform fast variational inference within this PGM to compute a posterior distribution over the complete attribute set. This posterior, rather than raw inputs, will condition a state-of-the-art transformer or diffusion-based generator via cross-attention mechanisms. The entire pipeline will be trained end-to-end, using a loss that combines the generative reconstruction loss with a regularization term from the PGM's inference objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified framework for joint inference and generation that explicitly reasons about attribute relationships. It will advance the field of controllable generation by providing a method to handle ambiguity robustly, moving beyond simple label conditioning. The integration offers a path towards more interpretable and user-aligned generative AI.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, trainable coupling between a structured symbolic PGM for coherent reasoning and a high-capacity neural generator, moving beyond using PGMs only for post-hoc analysis or as untrainable pre-processors. This fusion aims to combine the strengths of structured and deep learning paradigms for conditional generation.

proposals.title: Stability-Preserved Joint Training via Adaptive Gradient Equilibrium for Robust Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Jointly training generative models (e.g., VAEs) and discriminative models is a powerful paradigm for learning rich, transferable representations, as seen in prior work on self-supervised and multimodal learning. However, these components often have conflicting gradient updates, leading to training instability, mode collapse in the generative model, and suboptimal final performance. A principled method to maintain equilibrium between these competing objectives is a critical open problem in representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly aligning the gradient updates of the generative and discriminative components via projection-based gradient surgery, coupled with an adaptive loss re-weighting mechanism triggered by a stability monitor, will enable stable joint training and yield superior, more robust representations than standard adversarial or joint training methods.
proposals.method: We will design a system integrating a VAE with a discriminative network (e.g., a classifier or contrastive encoder). The core innovation is an Adaptive Gradient Equilibrium (AGE) module that performs projection-based gradient surgery on the discriminative model's updates to align them with the VAE's reconstruction and KL-divergence gradients. A stability monitor will track the VAE's reconstruction fidelity (PSNR/SSIM) and latent space statistics (e.g., KL divergence), triggering a dynamic re-weighting of the joint loss components via a soft, learnable gating mechanism to prevent generative quality degradation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, stable framework for joint generative-discriminative representation learning. We expect to demonstrate improved robustness and performance on downstream tasks (e.g., classification, few-shot learning) across image datasets. The proposed AGE module offers a general-purpose tool for mitigating gradient conflict in multi-objective learning systems.
proposals.novelty: While gradient surgery has been explored in multi-task learning, its application to align generative and discriminative objectives in a joint training framework is novel. The integration of a continuous stability monitor with adaptive loss re-weighting specifically designed to preserve generative model integrity moves beyond simple static loss balancing.

proposals.title: SymbioticVAE: Stabilizing Joint Generative-Discriminative Training via a Bidirectional Feedback Loop
proposals.motivation: Current methods for combining VAEs and discriminative models, such as multi-task learning or simple adversarial schemes, often lead to unstable training or representation collapse, where one component dominates. A stable, synergistic integration could yield models with both high-fidelity generative capabilities and robust, task-aligned representations, which is a core challenge in representation learning. Drawing inspiration from stabilized synthetic ecosystems in systems biology offers a novel framework to address this instability.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly engineering a bidirectional feedback loop between a VAE and a discriminative classifier—where each model's training signal directly and adaptively conditions the other's objective—we can achieve a stable co-training equilibrium that produces superior shared representations and improves both generative quality and discriminative performance.
proposals.method: We will implement a system where a discriminative classifier's task-loss gradients are used to adaptively re-weight the VAE's reconstruction loss per data point and to parameterize a task-dependent latent prior, steering the VAE to generate features salient for classification. Concurrently, the VAE will generate synthetic samples, with a focus on regions of high discriminative uncertainty or adversarial examples, to continually augment and challenge the classifier. Stability will be enforced via a novel consensus loss that penalizes divergence in the feature space, akin to metabolic cross-feeding dependencies that stabilize microbial consortia.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, stable training algorithm for joint generative-discriminative modeling, validated on image classification and generation benchmarks. It will provide new insights into controlling the dynamics of interacting neural networks, advancing beyond simple multi-task or adversarial setups. The resulting models are expected to exhibit improved sample efficiency, robustness, and interpretable, disentangled features aligned with the discriminative task.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the explicit design of a stabilizing, bidirectional feedback loop inspired by ecological cross-feeding, moving beyond sequential or parallel training. This contrasts with existing methods like VAEGANs or auxiliary classifier VAEs, which lack a mechanism for the discriminative model to continuously and adaptively shape the VAE's core generative objective during training.

proposals.title: JADE: Joint Adversarial Discriminative-Encoding for Robust and Transferable Representations
proposals.motivation: While generative models like VAEs learn rich data manifolds, their representations often lack the discriminative sharpness of supervised models. Conversely, purely discriminative models can be brittle and data-hungry. A unified framework that synergistically combines the complementary strengths of generative and discriminative learning could yield more robust, data-efficient, and semantically disentangled feature representations, a core challenge in representation learning pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint training objective combining a VAE's reconstruction and KL-divergence losses with a contrastive or supervised discriminative loss, mediated by an adversarial alignment mechanism, will produce latent representations that outperform either paradigm alone in terms of robustness, transferability, and interpretability.
proposals.method: We will design a hybrid architecture with a shared encoder. The decoder will define the generative VAE pathway. A discriminative pathway will consist of a projection head feeding into a contrastive (e.g., SimCLR-style) or supervised cross-entropy loss. Critically, we will introduce an adversarial module that encourages the latent space to be invariant to the training pathway, ensuring the shared representation is both generative and discriminative. Training will alternate between optimizing the joint objective (VAE + discriminative loss) and the adversarial alignment loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, unified framework for joint generative-discriminative representation learning, 2) empirical evidence showing improved robustness to adversarial examples and domain shift on benchmarks like ImageNet and DomainBed, and 3) analysis demonstrating more factorized and semantically meaningful latent spaces compared to standard VAEs or discriminative models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific adversarial alignment mechanism to fuse the two paradigms, moving beyond simple multi-task learning or using pre-trained discriminative models as fixed teachers, towards a truly co-adaptive and synergistic joint training process.

proposals.title: InverseDiffusion: Refining Visual Attribute Discovery via Diffusion Model Priors
proposals.motivation: Accurate extraction of fine-grained visual attributes from novel images is crucial for tasks like detailed image captioning, compositional reasoning, and controllable generation. However, existing supervised attribute predictors often fail on out-of-distribution data or novel compositions, and unsupervised discovery methods lack semantic grounding. The emergence of powerful, large-scale text-to-image diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) provides a rich, learned prior of visual concepts and their relationships, which remains underutilized for the inverse problem of attribute analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the generative prior embedded in a pre-trained text-conditioned diffusion model can be leveraged to iteratively refine and correct the attribute predictions from a base model, especially for novel or ambiguous images, by enforcing consistency between the predicted attributes and the visual evidence through a generative feedback loop.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, a base attribute predictor (e.g., a vision transformer) provides an initial set of attributes. Second, we employ a pre-trained, frozen diffusion model as a differentiable prior. We formulate an optimization loop where the initial attribute set is treated as a learnable embedding. This embedding is used to condition the diffusion model to reconstruct the input image via a denoising loss. The gradient from this reconstruction loss, which measures attribute-image consistency, is backpropagated to refine the attribute embedding. We will explore integrating self-attention maps from the diffusion U-Net to guide refinement towards salient regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel method for zero-shot and few-shot attribute refinement that leverages generative models as structured priors. It will advance the field by providing a new paradigm for solving inverse vision problems, moving beyond using generative models solely for synthesis. We will demonstrate improved performance on challenging zero-shot captioning and fine-grained attribute recognition benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are widely used for generation, using them as a *train-time, iterative refinement engine* for a discriminative attribute prediction task is novel. This approach differs from post-hoc captioning or simple distillation, as it creates a closed-loop, consistency-driven optimization between the attribute space and the image manifold.

proposals.title: Architecture-Agnostic Confidence Calibration: Extending Calibrated Training to Recurrent and Fully-Connected Networks
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning models, particularly in high-stakes applications like medical imaging or autonomous systems, require not only high accuracy but also reliable uncertainty estimates. While confidence calibration methods like label smoothing and temperature scaling have been studied for convolutional and transformer-based vision models, their efficacy and behavior in fundamentally different architectures like Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Fully-Connected Networks (FCNs) remain underexplored. Understanding calibration across architectures is crucial for deploying robust, interpretable models in sequential and tabular data domains where these networks are prevalent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that confidence-calibrated training approaches, which modify the training objective to produce better-calibrated outputs, will generalize effectively to RNNs and FCNs, but that the optimal strategy and its impact on performance will be architecture-dependent, requiring tailored adjustments.
proposals.method: We will adapt and systematically evaluate established calibration-aware training methods—specifically, label smoothing, focal loss, and explicit confidence penalty terms—on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks for sequential tasks (e.g., video activity recognition) and deep FCNs for tabular data. Using metrics like Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Brier score, we will benchmark against standard cross-entropy training and post-hoc temperature scaling. The core experiment will involve ablations on CIFAR-10 (using pixel sequences for RNNs), a time-series dataset, and a tabular benchmark (e.g., UCI datasets) to isolate architectural effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive analysis of in-training calibration for non-convolutional/transformer architectures, establishing best practices for building calibrated RNNs and FCNs. It will yield insights into how architectural inductive biases interact with calibration objectives, expanding the toolkit for reliable machine learning beyond computer vision.
proposals.novelty: While calibration is a known issue, prior work (e.g., Guo et al., 2017) focuses heavily on CNNs and post-hoc methods; investigating calibration-at-training-time specifically for RNNs and FCNs, and how it interacts with their unique learning dynamics, is a novel direction.

proposals.title: Unified Denoising and Distillation for Multimodal Sequence Modeling
proposals.motivation: Recent work, such as '3D Denoisers are Good 2D Teachers', demonstrates the power of denoising pretraining and cross-modal distillation for learning robust visual representations. However, the efficacy of this joint training framework remains largely unexplored for sequential and structured data domains like text and audio, which are fundamental to multimodal AI. Validating and adapting this paradigm beyond images is crucial for developing general-purpose, data-efficient multimodal models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint framework of denoising pretraining on a primary modality (e.g., audio spectrograms or text tokens) followed by cross-modal distillation to a secondary, aligned modality (e.g., text or semantic labels) will yield superior representations compared to unimodal or standard multimodal training, as measured by downstream task performance and sample efficiency.
proposals.method: We will adapt the core methodology from molecular pretraining to sequential data. For a primary domain (e.g., audio), we will pretrain a transformer or conformer encoder via a masked denoising objective on spectrogram patches. We will then perform cross-modal distillation to a pretrained text encoder (e.g., BERT or T5) using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) on paired audio-text data, aligning the learned representations. We will benchmark against supervised and self-supervised baselines on tasks like audio classification, retrieval, and captioning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic validation of the denoising-and-distillation framework for non-image modalities, establishing new strong baselines for audio and text representation learning. It will yield insights into the transferability of visual self-supervised principles and contribute a flexible, modular architecture for data-efficient multimodal sequence modeling.
proposals.novelty: While denoising and distillation are known separately, their combined, systematic application for pretraining sequential data encoders is novel. Extending a framework proven on 3D/2D molecular data to fundamentally different data types (sequential, variable-length) is a non-trivial and impactful generalization.

proposals.title: Adversarially Crafted Out-of-Distribution Examples for Improved Model Calibration
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning models, especially vision models, are often poorly calibrated, exhibiting overconfidence on out-of-distribution (OOD) data. This is a critical safety issue for real-world deployment. Existing calibration methods primarily focus on in-distribution data, leaving a gap in addressing systematic overconfidence under distribution shifts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly generating and training on adversarially crafted worst-case OOD samples will force a model to learn more conservative and better-calibrated confidence estimates across diverse inputs.
proposals.method: We will adapt adversarial training frameworks (e.g., PGD) to craft OOD perturbations that maximize predictive entropy or a calibration loss, rather than classification error. These crafted 'hard' OOD samples will be integrated into a training regimen alongside standard supervised data. We will leverage self-supervised representations (e.g., from contrastive pretraining) to guide the adversarial search towards semantically meaningful distribution shifts. The model will be trained with a combined loss of task performance and a calibration objective (e.g., Expected Calibration Error) on these adversarial OOD batches.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training methodology for improving model calibration under distribution shift. It will provide new insights into the relationship between adversarial robustness, OOD generalization, and predictive uncertainty. The resulting models are expected to be more reliable and trustworthy for safety-critical applications.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is used for robustness, and OOD detection is a separate field, the direct use of adversarial techniques to *generate* OOD data specifically for calibration training is novel. This bridges concepts from robustness and uncertainty estimation in a unique way.

proposals.title: One-Class Attention: Integrating Anomaly Detection Objectives into Self-Supervised Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Deep learning excels with abundant labeled data but struggles in low-data or anomaly detection regimes where only 'normal' class examples are plentiful. Classical one-class methods like SVMs and Isolation Forests offer principled geometric and isolation-based objectives for learning such representations, but they are often shallow and non-differentiable. Integrating their core principles into deep, self-supervised training could yield more robust and semantically meaningful representations for downstream tasks like industrial inspection or medical screening, where anomalies are rare.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating the geometric margin maximization of one-class SVMs and the isolation principles of Isolation Forests as auxiliary training objectives within a self-supervised learning framework will produce deep representations with improved separability between normal and anomalous data, enhancing performance on few-shot and zero-shot anomaly detection tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-objective training framework for a vision transformer encoder. The primary objective is a standard self-supervised task (e.g., masked image modeling). The auxiliary objectives are: 1) A differentiable one-class loss that maximizes the margin between projected representations of normal data and a learned center in a hypersphere, inspired by SVDD. 2) A novel 'isolation' loss that encourages the network to learn features where random subspace projections easily isolate samples, mimicking Isolation Forests. These losses will be applied to the [CLS] token representation, with adaptive weighting based on training dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid training paradigm that bridges classical anomaly detection with modern self-supervised deep learning. It will provide a new, more sample-efficient path for learning representations tailored for outlier detection, validated on benchmarks like MVTec AD and CIFAR-10/100. The method will also offer insights into the geometric properties of representations learned with anomaly-aware objectives.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses pre-trained networks for anomaly detection, our proposal novelly integrates the training objectives of classical one-class methods directly into the representation learning phase. The combination of hypersphere-based and isolation-based losses within a single self-supervised framework is unexplored and non-trivial, moving beyond simple fine-tuning or metric learning.

proposals.title: End-to-End Text-to-Image Synthesis via Jointly Trained Text-Image Representations
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art text-to-image models (e.g., DALL-E 2, Stable Diffusion) rely on frozen, pre-trained text encoders like CLIP. This creates a fundamental misalignment: the text encoder is optimized for contrastive image-text matching, not for the generative objective of synthesizing images. This suboptimal text representation can limit model expressiveness, coherence, and the ability to learn novel, domain-specific concepts from paired data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly training the text encoder and the image generator from scratch in an end-to-end manner will yield a more cohesive and task-aligned multimodal representation, leading to improved image fidelity, text-image alignment, and data efficiency compared to systems using fixed, pre-trained text encoders.
proposals.method: We propose a transformer-based architecture where a text encoder and a latent diffusion image generator are initialized randomly and trained jointly. The text encoder will use a causal or bidirectional transformer to produce a sequence of token embeddings. These embeddings will condition the diffusion model's denoising U-Net via cross-attention layers. The entire system will be trained end-to-end on large-scale image-text datasets (e.g., LAION) using the standard variational lower bound objective for diffusion models, with no frozen components. We will explore regularization techniques, such as a weak contrastive loss on the joint embeddings, to prevent representation collapse.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive analysis of end-to-end training for modern diffusion-based text-to-image models. It will deliver a new model capable of more tightly coupled text-image generation and a framework for learning domain-specific encoders without reliance on external, general-purpose models. The findings will challenge the prevailing paradigm of using fixed text encoders.
proposals.novelty: While joint training is classic, applying it to large-scale latent diffusion models with transformer-based text encoders is unexplored and non-trivial. The novelty lies in scaling this idea to state-of-the-art generative architectures and rigorously analyzing the emergent properties of the jointly learned representation space compared to the de facto CLIP-based standard.

proposals.title: Unpaired Cross-Modal Alignment via Self-Supervised Cycle Consistency and Noise-Contrastive Estimation
proposals.motivation: Large-scale, cleanly aligned text-image datasets are expensive and labor-intensive to curate, limiting the scalability and diversity of vision-language models. In contrast, vast, naturally unpaired collections of images and text documents are abundant on the web and in domain-specific repositories. Developing methods to learn effective joint representations from these unpaired collections would democratize multimodal learning and unlock new applications in specialized domains where paired data is scarce.
proposals.hypothesis: A model can learn semantically aligned cross-modal representations from unpaired data by enforcing consistency through a cycle-reconstruction objective and by leveraging within-modality self-supervised signals as an anchor for cross-modal alignment via noise-contrastive estimation.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stream architecture with image and text encoders. The core method involves two complementary objectives: (1) A cycle-consistency loss where a reconstructed image (from a text->image->text cycle) must match the original text's semantic content, using a pretrained image generator (e.g., a diffusion model) as a learned, noisy renderer, inspired by cross-modal distillation ideas. (2) A multimodal noise-contrastive estimation (NCE) loss that aligns representations by treating within-modality neighbors (found via self-supervised methods like MoCo or SimCSE) as positive pairs, effectively creating a pseudo-alignment graph across modalities. Attention mechanisms will be used to compute fine-grained similarity for the NCE loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable framework for training vision-language models without paired data, reducing dependency on curated datasets. It will advance the field by introducing a method that combines generative cycle consistency with discriminative contrastive learning for unpaired alignment. We will demonstrate its performance on zero-shot image captioning and retrieval tasks, particularly in data-scarce domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses contrastive learning on paired data (CLIP) or generative alignment within one modality, our approach uniquely combines a denoising cross-modal cycle-consistency objective (inspired by 3D-to-2D distillation) with self-supervised within-modality signals to create a training signal for unpaired multimodal data, which is a distinct and under-explored challenge.

proposals.title: Adversarial Textual Embedding Refinement (ATER): Improving Robustness and Disentanglement in Text Encoders
proposals.motivation: Modern vision-language models rely heavily on high-quality text representations, but these are often learned via standard contrastive or generative losses that may not fully capture robust, disentangled, and semantically rich features. Pre-2026, adversarial training has proven highly effective for improving robustness and feature quality in image domains, but its systematic application to text encoders, particularly within multimodal frameworks, remains underexplored. This gap presents an opportunity to enhance text encoders' resilience to noisy inputs and their ability to produce more generalizable and structured representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating adversarial objectives during the training of a text encoder will force it to learn more robust, disentangled, and semantically meaningful feature representations, leading to improved performance on downstream tasks such as zero-shot image captioning and cross-modal retrieval.
proposals.method: We will integrate a gradient-based adversarial training loop into the pre-training or fine-tuning of a transformer-based text encoder (e.g., a BERT or CLIP text tower). A perturbation generator will create small, semantically preserving adversarial noise in the token embedding space. The text encoder will be trained with a combined loss: a primary task loss (e.g., contrastive loss with image features or masked language modeling) and an adversarial loss from a discriminator network that tries to distinguish between clean and adversarially perturbed text features. We will explore both gradient sign method (FGSM) and Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) for perturbation generation, adapting them for the discrete text domain via embedding space attacks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for adversarially robust text representation learning. It will deliver empirical evidence on how adversarial training affects feature disentanglement and robustness metrics in text encoders. The resulting models are expected to show improved performance on zero-shot and retrieval-based benchmarks, contributing more reliable components for multimodal AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is common in vision, its targeted application to refine text encoder representations—especially by attacking the embedding space within a joint multimodal training objective—is a non-obvious extension. This approach moves beyond simple data augmentation, using an adversarial objective to directly shape the feature manifold.

proposals.title: Hi-Image: Hierarchical Autoregressive Modeling for Scalable High-Fidelity Image Generation
proposals.motivation: Current autoregressive models for image generation, such as PixelCNN and Image Transformer, treat pixels as a flat sequence, which scales poorly to high resolutions and struggles with long-range coherence. Hierarchical latent variable models like VQ-VAE-2 offer multi-scale representations but often rely on separate, non-autoregressive decoders. A unified hierarchical autoregressive framework, inspired by successes in multi-scale language modeling, is needed to efficiently capture global structure and local details for scalable, high-fidelity synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: Structuring an image generator with explicit multi-scale autoregressive layers, akin to hierarchical language models, will enable more efficient and coherent image generation by first modeling coarse semantic layout and then progressively refining details, outperforming flat-sequence baselines in both sample quality and computational efficiency.
proposals.method: We propose a transformer-based architecture where the image generation process is decomposed into a hierarchy of discrete latent codes, using a pre-trained VQ-GAN for tokenization. The model employs a top-down causal attention mask: a high-level transformer first autoregressively generates a low-resolution grid of semantic tokens, conditioning a second transformer that generates a higher-resolution grid, and so on. Crucially, cross-scale attention will allow higher-resolution layers to attend to the coarser tokens, integrating adaptive conditioning similar to guided reward mechanisms from prior work. Training will use a staged, supervised loss on the token sequences at each level.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new hierarchical autoregressive paradigm for image synthesis that bridges the benefits of discrete representation learning and structured sequence modeling. It will provide empirical evidence for the efficiency and quality gains of hierarchical modeling in vision, analogous to its success in NLP, and release a strong baseline for high-resolution image generation.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical models exist, applying a strictly autoregressive, multi-scale transformer with inter-layer causal attention to image tokens is novel. This approach directly transplants the hierarchical language modeling inductive bias into image generation, differing from prior VQ-VAE-2's non-autoregressive decoder or flat PixelCNN-style models.

proposals.title: Architectural Alignment via Representational Matching: Training Networks with CKA-Guided Losses
proposals.motivation: Understanding and controlling the internal representations of deep neural networks is a fundamental challenge in machine learning. While methods like Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) are powerful for analyzing representational similarity between trained networks, they are not used as an objective for *synthesizing* networks with desired representational properties. This limits our ability to design models with predictable internal dynamics or to distill specific representational structures from one network to another with a different architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CKA can be effectively used as a differentiable training loss to guide a student network's internal representations to match a predefined target structure (e.g., from a teacher network or a hand-designed template), even across disparate architectures, leading to networks with controllable and interpretable feature spaces.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework where a student network is optimized with a composite loss: a standard task loss (e.g., supervised classification) plus a CKA-based representational matching loss. The CKA loss will be computed between the student's layer activations and the target representations from a fixed teacher or a predefined template across a batch of data. We will employ attention-based architectures (aligning with the researcher's recent methods) for both student and teacher roles, and investigate distillation scenarios from 3D-aware models (e.g., denoisers) to 2D image networks, and vice-versa, inspired by cross-modal distillation themes. The optimization will require efficient, gradient-based approximations of CKA for large feature maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new paradigm for network training that prioritizes representational geometry, moving beyond just task performance. It will provide a practical tool for model compression, architecture-invariant knowledge transfer, and the systematic study of representation-function relationships. The resulting method could enable the creation of more interpretable and robust models by design.
proposals.novelty: While CKA is a standard analysis tool, its use as a primary, differentiable training objective for *inverse network engineering* is novel. This inverts the typical analysis pipeline, actively shaping internal representations rather than passively measuring them after training.

proposals.title: Decoupling Consistency: Learning Task-Aligned Similarity for Robust Self-Supervised and Semi-Supervised Learning
proposals.motivation: A core assumption in modern self-supervised and consistency regularization methods (e.g., SimCLR, BYOL, FixMatch) is that a network's own intermediate features provide a reliable perceptual similarity metric for defining positive pairs and consistency targets. This creates a circular dependency where the learner's evolving biases define its own learning signal, potentially entrenching spurious correlations and limiting generalization, especially under distribution shift or with limited labels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decoupling the similarity function used for consistency from the primary task network, and learning it in a task-aligned but representation-agnostic manner, will yield more robust and generalizable representations, particularly in low-data and cross-modal regimes.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-network framework with a primary encoder (F) and a separate, lightweight similarity network (S). S is trained via a meta-learning objective on a small held-out validation set or via cross-modal distillation (e.g., from CLIP's aligned image-text space) to predict perceptual/task-relevant similarity between data pairs. This learned similarity then guides the construction of positive pairs and the consistency loss (e.g., a contrastive or mean-squared-error loss) for training F, replacing the standard feature-based similarity. We will instantiate this within contrastive self-supervised pretraining and semi-supervised learning (SSL) benchmarks, using attention-based architectures for S to focus on task-relevant regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled critique and a practical alternative to the entrenched assumption of feature similarity consistency. We expect to demonstrate improved robustness to domain shift and better sample efficiency in SSL. The method offers a general plug-in for decoupling similarity learning in any consistency-based framework.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses fixed, pretrained networks (e.g., ImageNet classifiers) for perceptual loss, we propose to *learn* a adaptive, task-aligned similarity metric specifically to guide consistency, moving beyond a fixed prior. This directly challenges the self-referential learning loop at the heart of many modern representation learning methods.

proposals.title: Inverting the Bootstrap: A Probabilistic Generative Model for True Label Inference
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning relies on large, often noisy datasets. Label correction and bootstrapping methods attempt to refine these labels by iteratively incorporating model predictions, but this process can propagate and amplify model errors. A principled, model-based approach to infer the underlying true label distribution from noisy observations is a critical unsolved problem for robust representation learning, especially in self-supervised and semi-supervised settings prevalent in computer vision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the generative process from true latent labels to observed noisy labels and input data, we can directly infer a more accurate and calibrated true label posterior than iterative bootstrapping, leading to superior learned representations and downstream task performance.
proposals.method: We propose a latent variable model where the true label distribution is a latent variable conditioned on the input image. The observed (potentially noisy) label is generated via a noise corruption process. We will employ a variational inference framework, using an encoder network to approximate the posterior over true labels and a decoder to model the observation process. Training will maximize the evidence lower bound (ELBO), combining reconstruction of the observed label with a regularization term from the prior, encouraging the latent representation to capture the true semantic structure. Attention mechanisms will be integrated to model dependencies between image regions and label concepts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, probabilistic framework for learning from noisy labels that inverts the conventional bootstrapping paradigm. We expect to demonstrate improved robustness to label noise on standard benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR with synthetic noise) and more semantically coherent representations, evaluated via linear probe and fine-tuning performance. The method will offer a theoretically grounded alternative to heuristic label correction.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing methods that treat model predictions as improved labels in a bootstrapping loop, our approach directly models the true label as a latent variable and infers it probabilistically, decoupling the correction process from the current model's point estimates. This shifts the paradigm from iterative correction to joint inference.

proposals.title: Robust Multimodal Representation Learning via Adversarial Label Denoising and Cross-Modal Consistency
proposals.motivation: Modern self-supervised and multimodal learning methods achieve strong performance on curated datasets but are often brittle to realistic, structured label noise, which is prevalent in large-scale web-scraped data. Their robustness to adversarial or class-dependent label corruption, which can break semantic alignment across modalities, remains underexplored. This gap is critical for deploying reliable models in open-world applications like automated captioning or embodied AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint training framework combining a denoising objective for adversarial labels with cross-modal consistency regularization can learn representations that are significantly more robust to structured label noise than standard supervised or self-supervised baselines.
proposals.method: We will extend the denoising and cross-modal distillation principles from Lee's prior work (e.g., '3D Denoisers are Good 2D Teachers'). Specifically, we will corrupt image-text pairs with class-dependent and adversarial (e.g., targeted label-flip) noise patterns. A primary model will be trained with a hybrid loss: a noise-robust classification loss (e.g., Generalized Cross-Entropy) on the noisy labels and a contrastive cross-modal consistency loss (e.g., CLIP-style) between image and text embeddings. We will integrate an attention-based gating mechanism to dynamically weight samples based on estimated noise levels, drawing on Lee's expertise in attention and adaptive rewards.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic benchmark for multimodal model robustness under structured label noise. It will yield a novel training algorithm that integrates denoising with cross-modal alignment, advancing the field of robust representation learning. The findings will offer practical guidance for pretraining on noisy web data.
proposals.novelty: While label noise robustness has been studied in unimodal classification, its intersection with multimodal contrastive learning and adversarial corruption patterns is novel. The integration of a sample-weighting attention mechanism, informed by cross-modal agreement, offers a distinct approach from prior noise-robust methods.

proposals.title: Consistency Bootstrapping for Structured Visual Prediction: Extending to Semantic Segmentation and Human Pose Estimation
proposals.motivation: Consistency-based bootstrapping, as exemplified by methods like Mean Teacher and Noisy Student, has shown remarkable success in semi-supervised learning for classification and object detection by enforcing prediction invariance to perturbations. However, its application to other dense, structured prediction tasks like semantic segmentation and human pose estimation remains underexplored. These tasks are crucial for scene understanding but require large amounts of expensive pixel- or keypoint-level annotations, creating a significant bottleneck for model training.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a tailored consistency bootstrapping framework, which enforces stability across structured outputs under strong data augmentations and model perturbations, can effectively leverage unlabeled data to improve performance and data efficiency in semantic segmentation and human pose estimation.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the Mean Teacher framework to structured outputs. For a given input image, the student model (e.g., a DeepLabV3+ or HRNet backbone) will process a strongly augmented version, while the teacher model (an EMA of the student) processes a weakly augmented version. The consistency loss will be computed between the teacher's and student's structured output maps—either segmentation logits or heatmaps for keypoints—using a loss like KL divergence or Mean Squared Error. We will experiment with task-specific augmentation strategies and consistency weighting schedules, integrating this semi-supervised objective with standard supervised losses on labeled data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic study and a practical framework for applying consistency bootstrapping to major structured prediction tasks, reducing their reliance on vast labeled datasets. We expect to demonstrate significant performance gains on standard benchmarks like PASCAL VOC and COCO under low-label regimes, contributing a new strong baseline for semi-supervised structured learning.
proposals.novelty: While consistency training is established for classification, its direct extension to the structured, spatially-correlated outputs of segmentation and pose estimation is non-trivial and requires careful design of perturbation strategies and loss functions tailored to the output geometry, moving beyond simple per-pixel classification.

proposals.title: Bootstrapped Active Label Cleaning: Efficient Data Curation via Self-Training and Uncertainty-Acquisition
proposals.motivation: Large-scale datasets are often noisy, degrading model performance and requiring expensive manual verification. Pre-2026, methods like noisy-label robust training (e.g., bootstrapping) and active learning for label acquisition exist, but they are typically applied separately. An integrated framework is needed to efficiently identify and correct the most impactful label errors within a fixed annotation budget.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained with a robust bootstrapping loss can generate more reliable uncertainty estimates and pseudo-labels, which, when used to guide an active learning acquisition function, will select a more informative subset of samples for human verification, leading to superior data-cleaning efficiency and final model performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a unified iterative framework. First, we train a vision model (e.g., a Vision Transformer) on a noisy dataset using a generalized bootstrapping loss that blends the noisy label with the model's own prediction. This yields a robust teacher model. In each active learning cycle, we use this model to compute per-sample uncertainty (e.g., predictive entropy) and a confidence-weighted consistency score between its prediction and the original noisy label. We then design a joint acquisition function prioritizing high-uncertainty, low-consistency samples for human annotation. The cleaned labels are used to refine the model via supervised fine-tuning and the bootstrapping loss for the next cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical algorithm for cost-effective dataset curation. It will provide new insights into how robust training paradigms can enhance active learning criteria beyond standard uncertainty sampling. We will validate our method on established noisy-label benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10N, WebVision) and a real-world image captioning task derived from NICE challenge data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, synergistic coupling of the bootstrapping mechanism—traditionally used only for noise robustness—directly into the active learning acquisition logic, creating a closed-loop system where the model's self-training signal actively guides its own data cleaning.

proposals.title: Robust Visual Representation Learning via Adaptive Self-Supervision on Noisy Web-Scale Datasets
proposals.motivation: Training vision models on massive, heuristically labeled web datasets is crucial for scaling AI capabilities, but label noise and distributional biases severely degrade model performance and robustness. Pre-2026 methods like CLIP and self-supervised learning show promise but often struggle with the extreme noise and long-tail distributions inherent in web-scale data, limiting their reliability for downstream tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a training framework combining adaptive self-supervised objectives with a cross-modal distillation mechanism can learn robust visual representations from millions of noisy image-text pairs, outperforming standard supervised and contrastive pre-training on downstream zero-shot and fine-tuning benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will extend the denoising and cross-modal distillation principles from molecular pretraining to the vision-language domain. The core method involves a two-stream architecture: a primary vision transformer trained with a novel adaptive masked autoencoding loss that weights reconstruction based on estimated label confidence, and a momentum teacher network providing stabilized targets. We will integrate a reward-guided sampling strategy, inspired by adaptive multimodal rewards, to prioritize training on informative or clean data subsets, mitigating the impact of noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, noise-robust pre-training recipe for web-scale data, validated on zero-shot image classification and captioning tasks. It will provide new insights into effectively combining self-supervised denoising, distillation, and adaptive sampling for learning from imperfect large-scale supervision.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic adaptation of denoising distillation (from 3D-to-2D) and adaptive reward guidance to the specific problem of web-scale visual pre-training, creating a unified framework that dynamically modulates learning signals based on data quality.

proposals.title: Pre-Embedded Language Modeling: Improving Efficiency and Performance via Initialization with Large-Scale Word Embeddings
proposals.motivation: Training large language models (LLMs) from scratch on character or subword tokens is computationally expensive and data-hungry. This process requires the model to learn both fundamental semantic representations and high-level linguistic structures simultaneously. Leveraging pre-existing, high-quality word embeddings (e.g., GloVe, fastText) as a fixed or fine-tunable initialization could significantly reduce training time and data requirements while potentially improving final model performance, especially in low-resource or multimodal scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: Initializing the embedding layer of a transformer-based language model with static, large-scale pre-trained word embeddings, rather than training it from random character/subword initialization, will lead to faster convergence, improved sample efficiency, and superior final performance on downstream tasks like image captioning and visual question answering.
proposals.method: We will initialize the embedding matrix of a transformer decoder (e.g., a GPT-2/3 style architecture) with pre-trained 300-dimensional GloVe or fastText vectors, mapping out-of-vocabulary tokens to an average embedding. This model will be trained using a standard autoregressive objective on a multimodal corpus (e.g., Conceptual Captions). We will compare against an identical model trained from scratch, monitoring convergence speed, perplexity, and zero-shot/finetuned performance on NICE challenge-style captioning and VQA benchmarks. An adaptive variant where embeddings are fine-tuned via a learned weighted residual connection will also be explored.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical analysis of the benefits of word-level semantic priors in modern LLM training. It will deliver a more efficient and effective training recipe for language and vision-language models. The findings could redefine standard initialization practices, particularly for resource-constrained research or domains with limited textual data.
proposals.novelty: While pre-trained embeddings are classic, their systematic integration and evaluation in the context of large-scale transformer-based language and multimodal models, trained with contemporary self-supervised objectives, remains underexplored compared to the dominant paradigm of training subword embeddings from scratch.

proposals.title: Dynamic Modality Fusion with Consistency Regularization for Robust Multimodal Learning
proposals.motivation: Multimodal learning often relies on side information like structured attributes and unstructured text, but naively fusing them can lead to one modality dominating or providing contradictory signals, harming generalization. Prior work in cross-modal distillation and adaptive rewards highlights the need for more intelligent fusion mechanisms. This project addresses the core challenge of learning complementary, non-redundant representations from heterogeneous data sources for downstream tasks like zero-shot recognition or captioning.
proposals.hypothesis: A fusion architecture with an instance-wise dynamic gating mechanism, regularized by a modality-consistency loss, will learn more robust and generalizable representations by ensuring attribute and language modalities provide truly complementary information.
proposals.method: We will design separate encoders for attribute vectors (e.g., MLP) and raw text (e.g., transformer). Their outputs will feed into a cross-modal gating module, inspired by adaptive attention, that produces input-dependent fusion weights. A key innovation is an auxiliary consistency loss, potentially using a contrastive or KL-divergence objective, applied to intermediate representations to minimize semantic contradiction. The entire system will be trained end-to-end with a primary task loss (e.g., classification) plus the consistency regularizer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for fusing structured and unstructured side information, improving model robustness and zero-shot generalization. It will advance multimodal learning by introducing a trainable, consistency-constrained fusion mechanism, with empirical validation on challenging benchmarks like zero-shot image captioning and attribute-based recognition.
proposals.novelty: While dynamic fusion exists, jointly learning with an explicit consistency loss to *enforce complementarity* between attribute and language modalities is novel. This moves beyond simple concatenation or attention to actively shape the joint representation space.

proposals.title: OntoGen: Ontology-Guided Latent Space Factorization for Compositional Visual Generation
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale generative models (e.g., diffusion models, autoregressive transformers) learn from unstructured text-image pairs, which often fails to capture the systematic compositional structure of complex object categories (e.g., vehicles, furniture). This leads to incoherent generations for rare or novel categories and limits fine-grained, controllable synthesis based on part-whole relationships. Grounding generation in explicit, structured knowledge is a critical step toward more systematic generalization and interpretable control.
proposals.hypothesis: By factorizing a generative model's latent space according to ontological relationships (is-a, part-of, has-property) and using these factors as conditioning signals during training, we can achieve more semantically consistent and controllable generation for structurally diverse object categories, especially those with limited data.
proposals.method: We will develop a layered conditional diffusion model. A structured encoder will map an input ontology subgraph (from ConceptNet/WordNet) for a target category into a set of disentangled latent vectors representing category, parts, and attributes. These vectors will condition a U-Net denoiser via cross-attention and adaptive layer normalization, similar to techniques in multimodal reward shaping. Training will use a hybrid objective: a standard image denoising loss on datasets like PartNet or COCO, and a distillation loss aligning the model's internal representations with pre-trained visual features of object parts, inspired by cross-modal distillation methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for knowledge-guided generative modeling that explicitly leverages semantic structure. It will demonstrate improved generation quality and controllability for complex, structured object categories, providing a path toward more data-efficient and systematic visual concept learning. The released model and code will serve as a benchmark for structured visual reasoning in generation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses text or class labels for conditioning, our method explicitly factors the conditioning and latent space using a full ontological graph, enabling direct control over part-level and attribute-level semantics during generation, which is a non-trivial extension of current multimodal fusion techniques.

proposals.title: Ambiguity-Aware Multimodal Completion for Scalable Image Retrieval
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale image retrieval systems, powered by contrastive models like CLIP, excel at matching precise text queries but fail on ambiguous, partial, or contradictory descriptions, which are common in real user interactions. This brittleness limits the utility of web-scale search, as it requires users to formulate perfect prompts. A system that can infer and complete missing or noisy attributes from the data distribution would enable more intuitive, human-like search and open new applications in creative and analytical tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A retrieval model trained with an explicit ambiguity-aware objective can learn to perform probabilistic attribute completion from partial queries, enabling it to retrieve more relevant and diverse images from a billion-scale index compared to standard exact-matching models.
proposals.method: We will train a dual-encoder model (image and text) using a large web dataset. The core innovation is a novel training objective combining: 1) standard contrastive loss for alignment, 2) a masked attribute modeling loss on the text encoder to learn completion, and 3) a consistency loss where the model must retrieve similar images for a full query and its ambiguous, partial counterpart. For inference, we will use efficient approximate nearest neighbor search (e.g., FAISS) on the learned embeddings, where the text encoder's output for an ambiguous query represents a 'completed' semantic vector.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new training paradigm for scalable multimodal retrieval that is robust to query ambiguity. It will provide a publicly available model and benchmark for 'retrieval with completion,' advancing the field beyond strict matching. The system demonstrates how generative reasoning can be integrated into efficient retrieval pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses retrieval for generation or trains on noisy data, explicitly training a retrieval model to perform probabilistic attribute completion from imperfect queries is novel. This moves the system's capability from recall to inference, blending discriminative and generative modeling for scalable search.

proposals.title: MAML for Metrics: Meta-Learning Context-Aware Distance Functions for Adaptive Visual Recognition
proposals.motivation: Current metric learning methods produce a static embedding space, limiting their applicability to tasks requiring different semantic granularities, such as fine-grained species classification versus coarse animal categorization. This rigidity fails to capture the contextual and goal-dependent nature of human similarity judgments, leading to poor generalization when task definitions shift. A model that can dynamically adapt its notion of similarity based on context or instruction would be more robust and aligned with practical, multi-faceted recognition needs.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework, trained on a distribution of related tasks defined at different semantic scales, can learn to generate or select a context-conditioned distance function that warps the embedding geometry to optimize for variable downstream objectives.
proposals.method: We propose a model with a shared feature encoder (e.g., a Vision Transformer) and a context-aware metric generator, implemented as an attention-based hypernetwork. The system is meta-trained using a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) paradigm on a constructed distribution of tasks, where each task is defined by a specific granularity of classification (e.g., breed vs. species). The inner-loop adaptation rapidly adjusts the metric generator's parameters given a context signal (e.g., a class hierarchy or a textual prompt), and the outer-loop updates the shared encoder and generator to enable fast adaptation to new tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a single, flexible embedding model capable of producing multiple, semantically coherent similarity metrics from a single forward pass. It will provide a principled meta-optimization framework for learning adaptive metrics, moving beyond handcrafted or ensemble-based approaches. The resulting model will demonstrate improved robustness and generalization in zero-shot and few-shot scenarios where the relevant notion of similarity is context-dependent.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been used for fast adaptation of classifiers, its application to dynamically generate the core distance function of a metric learning system based on a contextual signal is novel. This integrates the flexibility of hypernetworks and the generalization power of MAML directly into the metric learning objective.

proposals.title: Noisy Channel Distillation: Assembling High-Fidelity Training Corpora from Web-Scale Noisy Image-Text Pairs
proposals.motivation: Scaling vision-language models with web-scale, loosely aligned image-text pairs is crucial for performance, but the inherent noise in these datasets limits learning efficiency and final model quality. Current filtering methods, such as CLIP score thresholding, are blunt instruments that discard potentially useful data and do not constructively assemble a coherent training signal. A more sophisticated, assembly-inspired approach is needed to maximize the utility of noisy web data for training robust joint embedding models.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a bioinformatics-inspired 'overlap-layout-consensus' pipeline to web-scale noisy image-text data, we can distill a high-alignment training corpus that enables more sample-efficient scaling and superior downstream performance compared to training on raw or crudely filtered data.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-stage filtering pipeline. First, an 'overlap' stage uses weak consistency heuristics (e.g., CLIP similarity, object detector confidence, BLIP caption consistency) to generate multiple alignment scores per pair. Second, a 'layout' stage employs a lightweight, trainable gating network to weight and combine these scores into a unified reliability metric. Finally, a 'consensus' stage uses this metric to weight samples during contrastive loss computation or to select a high-confidence subset, creating a dynamically distilled training curriculum. The core joint embedding model will be a ViT-B/16 transformer with a cross-modal attention-based projection head, trained with a standard InfoNCE loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled framework for data curation at scale, moving beyond simple filtering to constructive assembly. It will deliver a new state-of-the-art in data-efficient vision-language pretraining and offer insights into the relative importance of different alignment signals for model learning.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the conceptual and algorithmic adaptation of genome assembly techniques—specifically the overlap-layout-consensus paradigm—to the problem of distilling coherence from noisy multimodal data, a direct analogy not previously explored in machine learning.

proposals.title: Robust Joint Embeddings via Noisy Web-Scale Pretraining and Cross-Modal Attention Refinement
proposals.motivation: Learning from billions of noisy web image-text pairs is crucial for scaling vision-language models, but existing methods like CLIP can degrade with severe misalignment. This limits the utility of the largest, most diverse datasets. Developing robust training methods that can handle this noise is a key open problem for achieving general-purpose multimodal understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a training framework combining a denoising pretext task with a cross-modal attention distillation mechanism can learn significantly more robust joint embeddings from noisy web data than standard contrastive loss alone.
proposals.method: We will scale up a dual-encoder architecture (ViT/ResNet + Transformer) trained on millions of noisy pairs. The core method integrates two components: 1) A self-supervised denoising loss on the image encoder, inspired by 3D-to-2D distillation, to learn cleaner visual features. 2) An adaptive cross-modal attention module that learns to re-weight caption tokens relative to the image, distilling a 'soft alignment' signal to mitigate noise, extending ideas from reward-guided learning. Training will use a weighted sum of contrastive, denoising, and attention distillation losses.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable training framework for noise-robust vision-language representation learning. It will provide new state-of-the-art benchmarks on zero-shot image classification and retrieval tasks using noisy pretraining data, and offer insights into the synergy between denoising and cross-modal attention.
proposals.novelty: While denoising and attention are known separately, their combined use as a targeted solution for web-scale noise in joint embedding training is novel. The cross-modal attention refinement mechanism, trained via distillation without clean alignment labels, is a non-obvious extension of adaptive reward and teacher-student frameworks.

proposals.title: Inverse Generation in Joint Embedding Space: Physics-Constrained Decoding for Fine-Grained Image Captioning
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models for image captioning often produce generic or hallucinated descriptions, lacking fine-grained detail and strict visual grounding. This limits their utility in critical applications like scientific documentation or assistive technology. The problem mirrors geophysical inverse problems, where sparse data must be used to infer a detailed, physically-consistent model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treating fine-grained caption generation as an inverse problem within a pre-trained joint image-text embedding space, and solving it via a decoding process constrained by forward-compatibility to the image, will yield more detailed, accurate, and visually faithful textual descriptions than standard autoregressive decoding.
proposals.method: We will leverage a large-scale, pre-trained joint embedding model (e.g., CLIP or ALIGN) as our constrained 'physics' space. Starting from an image embedding, we will decode text iteratively using a method inspired by iterative denoising or gradient-based optimization in the latent space of a large language model. The decoding objective will combine standard language modeling likelihood with a regularization term that minimizes the distance between the generated text's embedding and the original image embedding in the joint space, enforcing forward-compatibility. We will explore integrating attention mechanisms from the image encoder to guide the decoding of specific regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, constrained decoding paradigm for vision-language generation, moving beyond standard likelihood maximization. It will provide a new benchmark for fine-grained, hallucination-resistant image captioning. The formal analogy to inverse problems offers a principled framework for reasoning about grounding and fidelity in generative models.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual and methodological transfer of inverse problem-solving from computational physics to vision-language generation, explicitly using the joint embedding space as a constraint manifold. This contrasts with existing methods that primarily rely on supervised training of sequence-to-sequence models without an explicit inverse-compatibility loss.

proposals.title: Scaling Cross-Modal Feature Learning: A Large-Scale Benchmark and Analysis on Diverse Audio-Visual Datasets
proposals.motivation: Cross-modal self-supervised learning, particularly for audio-visual data, has shown promise on constrained datasets like CUAVE and AVLetters. However, the scalability and generalizability of these methods to larger, more diverse, and 'in-the-wild' datasets remain an open question. Validating on such datasets is crucial for transitioning these techniques from controlled benchmarks to real-world applications like assistive technologies and embodied AI, where data is inherently noisy and heterogeneous.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cross-modality feature learning methods, particularly those leveraging self-supervised objectives and attention-based fusion, will demonstrate improved robustness and more generalizable representations when trained and evaluated on large-scale, diverse audio-visual datasets, but will also reveal specific failure modes tied to dataset bias and modality alignment complexity.
proposals.method: We will extend a state-of-the-art cross-modal framework, such as a contrastive or masked modeling architecture with multimodal transformer attention, for joint audio-visual representation learning. The core experiment involves systematic training and evaluation on a curated suite of large-scale datasets (e.g., AudioSet, VGGSound, How2, and Ego4D) spanning diverse domains from generic sounds to egocentric video and instructional speech. We will perform rigorous ablation studies on model scale, pretraining objectives, and attention-based fusion mechanisms, benchmarking against supervised baselines and prior self-supervised methods on downstream tasks like action recognition, audio event detection, and cross-modal retrieval.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark of cross-modal self-supervised learning across a spectrum of large-scale audio-visual datasets, establishing new baselines for the community. It will yield actionable insights into the data requirements, architectural choices, and limitations of current methods for scaling to diverse real-world data.
proposals.novelty: While prior work validates methods on single or small-scale datasets, our systematic large-scale benchmarking across intentionally diverse domains is novel. The analysis will uniquely identify which aspects of representation learning transfer and which are dataset-specific, moving beyond mere performance reporting to a diagnostic understanding of scalability.

proposals.title: Depth and Diversity in Multimodal Fusion: Architectural Scaling Laws for Cross-Modal Alignment
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models, such as CLIP and ALIGN, rely on standard Transformer or ResNet backbones with relatively shallow fusion mechanisms. It is unclear how the depth and architectural composition of the fusion network impact the quality of learned joint representations, especially for tasks requiring fine-grained cross-modal reasoning like zero-shot captioning or retrieval. A systematic study is needed to move beyond empirical scaling of parameters and understand the principles of architectural scaling for multimodal feature learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deeper, more heterogeneous fusion architectures—specifically those combining self-attention with alternative layer types like gated linear units (GLUs) or dynamic convolutions—will yield significant improvements in cross-modal alignment performance and sample efficiency compared to standard, uniformly deep Transformers.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled experimental framework based on a dual-encoder model with a cross-modal fusion transformer. We will systematically vary the depth (number of layers) and layer diversity (e.g., interleaving standard multi-head self-attention with Gated Attention Units or Perceiver-style cross-attention layers) within the fusion network. Training will follow a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) on large-scale image-text pairs, with evaluation on zero-shot tasks from the NICE challenge suite and retrieval benchmarks. We will analyze performance scaling, training dynamics, and the emergent properties of the learned representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical scaling laws and architectural guidelines for building more efficient and powerful multimodal fusion networks. It will contribute a new family of fusion architectures and a deeper understanding of how depth and layer composition affect the learning of joint embeddings, directly impacting future model design for vision-language tasks.
proposals.novelty: While scaling model size is common, a principled investigation into the depth and layer-type diversity of the *fusion module itself* is underexplored. This moves beyond simply making encoders deeper and instead focuses on the architectural design of the network that performs cross-modal integration, a critical bottleneck.

proposals.title: M3AE-2: Scaling Masked Multimodal Autoencoders to Trillion-Token Web Video
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models are primarily trained on static image-text pairs, lacking the rich temporal and auditory context of real-world experiences. Scaling to internet-scale multimodal video data is the next frontier for learning robust, unified world representations, but presents immense computational and algorithmic challenges in efficient training and cross-modal alignment. This work directly addresses the core scaling bottlenecks for learning from the next generation of web data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a massively scaled, asymmetric encoder-decoder architecture trained with a unified masked multimodal reconstruction objective on a trillion-token corpus of video, audio, and transcribed speech will learn superior, temporally coherent representations that significantly improve zero-shot performance on diverse downstream tasks compared to models trained on image-text data alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the Masked Multimodal Autoencoder (M3AE) framework to the video domain. The model will use a large, frozen visual encoder (e.g., ViT-G) and a trainable, massive multimodal decoder with cross-attention to process sequences of masked video patches, audio spectrogram tokens, and text tokens. Training will employ a unified denoising objective across all modalities on a newly curated dataset of 100M+ web videos with automatic speech recognition transcripts. Key innovations include a novel adaptive masking strategy that prioritizes semantically critical frames and a cross-modal distillation loss from a pre-trained 3D denoiser to guide the learning of temporal features.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a scalable recipe for training trillion-parameter models on web-scale multimodal sequences, 2) the M3AE-2 model and a large curated video dataset, and 3) state-of-the-art benchmarks in zero-shot video understanding, audio-visual retrieval, and multimodal reasoning, demonstrating the value of unified, large-scale pretraining on raw video.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales image-text models or uses curated video datasets, this proposal uniquely focuses on scaling a *reconstruction-based* pretraining paradigm to raw, uncurated web video with audio, leveraging insights from denoising teachers and adaptive rewards to overcome the noise and complexity of this data source.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Modality Bottleneck: Learning Explicit Modality-Specific Factors from Shared Multimodal Representations
proposals.motivation: Multimodal learning has focused on extracting shared representations, but downstream tasks often require modality-specific information, such as visual texture or acoustic timbre, which is lost in a purely shared latent space. This limits the adaptability and interpretability of models for tasks like cross-modal retrieval or modality-specific editing. A method to reliably disentangle and recover these modality-specific factors from a shared representation is a critical missing capability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modality-specific information can be explicitly recovered from a shared multimodal representation via a learned, invertible disentanglement, and that this recovered information will improve performance on tasks requiring fine-grained, modality-aware reasoning compared to using only the shared representation.
proposals.method: We will train a multimodal encoder (e.g., a vision-language model) to produce a shared representation. A novel, lightweight disentanglement module with modality-specific branches will then learn to reconstruct the original modality inputs from this shared code. We will enforce separation via a combination of reconstruction losses, a contrastive loss that discourages shared information in the modality-specific branches, and an information bottleneck on the shared representation. Architecturally, we will explore cross-modal attention mechanisms to guide the disentanglement, inspired by recent self-attention based methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for explicit modality disentanglement, extending the utility of shared representations. We will contribute new benchmarks for evaluating modality-specific information retention and demonstrate improved performance on tasks like zero-shot image captioning (requiring fine-grained visual details) and cross-modal retrieval with modality-specific queries.
proposals.novelty: While prior work disentangles content from style within a single modality, our approach inverts the multimodal learning paradigm by explicitly extracting *modality-specific* factors from a *shared* representation, a direction less explored than learning the shared space itself. The use of adaptive, cross-modal attention for guiding this separation is a novel technical strategy.

proposals.title: Decomposed Video Transformer (DeViT): Self-Supervised Learning via Disentangled Motion-Content Attention
proposals.motivation: Learning effective video representations is crucial for action recognition and understanding, but remains challenging due to data inefficiency and the entanglement of scene content and motion dynamics. Pre-2026 self-supervised methods like contrastive learning or masked autoencoding often learn entangled features, which can be suboptimal for tasks requiring explicit reasoning about motion. A decompositional approach could yield more interpretable, robust, and data-efficient representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly disentangling motion and content features within a self-supervised transformer framework will yield video representations that are more transferable, robust to spurious correlations, and superior for downstream tasks like fine-grained action classification compared to entangled representations.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stream transformer encoder architecture with a novel decomposed self-attention mechanism. One stream processes RGB frames to capture content, while another processes temporal differences or optical flow for motion. A cross-stream attention module will enforce separation, using a contrastive loss to distinguish motion features from content features and a reconstruction loss (inspired by denoising autoencoders) to ensure the combined features retain full information. The model will be pre-trained on large-scale video datasets (e.g., Kinetics) without labels, using a combination of masked frame modeling and temporal contrastive objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel self-supervised video model with an inductive bias for feature disentanglement, 2) a rigorous analysis showing the benefits of decomposed features for downstream task performance and robustness, and 3) state-of-the-art or competitive results on major video action classification benchmarks (e.g., UCF101, HMDB51, Something-Something v2).
