proposals.novelty: While decomposition has been explored in generative video models, its integration into a discriminative, self-supervised transformer via a specialized attention mechanism for representation learning is novel. This moves beyond simply using two-stream inputs to actively disentangle representations within a unified pre-training objective.

proposals.title: Disentangled Motion-Content Representations via Adversarial Consistency for Video Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current video representation learning methods often conflate motion and content factors, limiting their interpretability and transferability to downstream tasks like action recognition or video captioning. Disentangling these factors is a long-standing challenge in computer vision, with clear benefits for controllable generation and robust understanding. Pre-2026, techniques from generative models like beta-VAE and adversarial training offer promising but underexplored pathways for achieving this separation in a self-supervised setting.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating an adversarial consistency objective with a beta-VAE-style bottleneck into a self-supervised video learning framework, we can learn representations where motion and content factors are more effectively disentangled, leading to improved performance and generalization on video understanding tasks.
proposals.method: We will build upon a contrastive or masked autoencoder video backbone (e.g., a ViT). The model will encode input video clips into separate motion and content latent codes via a split encoder. A beta-VAE KL-divergence penalty will be applied to encourage factor independence. Crucially, we will introduce an adversarial discriminator trained to classify the factor type (motion vs. content), while the encoder is trained to maximize the discriminator's uncertainty, enforcing factor separation. The overall objective will combine this adversarial loss with a self-supervised reconstruction or contrastive loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, effective method for learning disentangled video representations without dense supervision. It will advance the field by demonstrating how adversarial principles from generative modeling can be integrated with modern self-supervised learning for structured representation learning. We expect to show quantifiable improvements on benchmarks for action recognition, video retrieval, and zero-shot transfer.
proposals.novelty: While disentanglement has been studied in static images, its application to video with modern self-supervised backbones (e.g., masked autoencoders) is nascent. The key novelty is the adversarial consistency mechanism specifically designed to separate temporal (motion) and spatial (content) factors within a single, end-to-end trainable framework for video.

proposals.title: End-to-End Joint Embedding Learning with Adaptive Compatibility and Cross-Modal Attention
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models often rely on fixed, pre-extracted image features from models like CLIP, which creates a suboptimal, disjointed pipeline. This separation limits the model's ability to adaptively refine visual representations for specific downstream tasks, such as zero-shot captioning or retrieval, where the compatibility function is crucial. An end-to-end framework that jointly optimizes the feature extractor and compatibility function is needed to achieve more aligned and powerful multimodal representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an end-to-end trainable framework, which jointly learns a task-adaptive image encoder and a parametric compatibility function, will yield a more cohesive and higher-performing joint embedding space than methods using fixed visual features. This approach will particularly improve performance on fine-grained cross-modal tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a transformer-based architecture where a visual encoder (e.g., a ViT) and a text encoder are trained from scratch or fine-tuned end-to-end. The core innovation is a learnable, multi-head cross-modal attention module that serves as the adaptive compatibility function, computing similarity between image patch tokens and text tokens. This module replaces fixed cosine similarity, allowing the model to discover nuanced alignments. Training will leverage a combination of contrastive loss (like InfoNCE) and generative captioning loss, inspired by recent denoising and distillation methods, to ensure robust representation learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, flexible framework for learning joint multimodal spaces that breaks the dependency on fixed pre-trained features. It will demonstrate significant gains on benchmarks for zero-shot image captioning and cross-modal retrieval. The proposed adaptive compatibility module offers a generalizable component for improving alignment in other multimodal tasks.
proposals.novelty: While joint training is known, our method's novelty lies in explicitly replacing the fixed compatibility metric with a trainable, attention-based module within an end-to-end framework, enabling the visual features to be dynamically shaped by the textual context and the compatibility objective. This moves beyond simply fine-tuning a pre-trained encoder with a linear probe.

proposals.title: Multi-Task Self-Supervision with Adaptive Attention: Learning Robust Image Representations via Joint Denoising, Distillation, and Captioning
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised learning (SSL) methods for image representation often rely on a single pretext task, which may yield features biased towards that task's specific invariances, limiting generalization. Multi-task learning (MTL) offers a promising path to more robust and transferable representations by leveraging complementary signals from diverse, related tasks. However, effectively balancing and fusing information from tasks with different modalities and objectives—like pixel reconstruction, cross-modal distillation, and language generation—remains a significant challenge in pre-2026 literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly training a shared visual encoder on a carefully selected suite of self-supervised tasks—including 2D image denoising, cross-modal distillation from 3D models, and zero-shot image captioning—will produce more generalizable and semantically rich image representations than single-task SSL. We further hypothesize that an adaptive attention-based gating mechanism can dynamically manage task interference and synergy.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-task framework with a shared Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone. The model will be trained concurrently on: 1) a 2D denoising objective, distilling knowledge from a pre-trained 3D denoiser as a teacher; 2) a contrastive image-text alignment task using noisy student distillation from a large vision-language model; and 3) a lightweight, prefix-based captioning head for zero-shot caption generation. A novel adaptive multi-head attention gate, inspired by mixture-of-experts, will be inserted between ViT blocks to learn task-specific feature modulation, allowing the shared backbone to specialize its representations dynamically per task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel MTL framework for self-supervised visual representation learning that systematically combines diverse supervisory signals. We expect to demonstrate improved performance on downstream tasks like image classification, detection, and zero-shot retrieval compared to state-of-the-art single-task SSL baselines. The proposed adaptive gating mechanism will offer insights into managing task relationships in complex MTL setups.
proposals.novelty: While MTL and SSL are well-studied independently, their combination for foundational image model training via a synergistic trio of denoising, cross-modal distillation, and captioning is novel. The introduction of an adaptive attention-based gating mechanism specifically designed to handle the distinct natures of these tasks within a shared transformer represents a significant architectural innovation over static weight-sharing or simple loss balancing.

proposals.title: Dual-Stream Embedding Inversion: Generating Prototypical Images from Class Embeddings via Cross-Modal Distillation
proposals.motivation: Modern vision systems excel at mapping images to semantic embeddings, but the inverse process—generating or retrieving a canonical image from a class embedding—remains underexplored. This capability is crucial for interpretability, dataset distillation, and providing visual feedback in human-in-the-loop systems. Current methods, like GAN inversion or text-to-image models, are not optimized for this specific, constrained task of producing a single, representative prototype from a learned embedding space.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual-stream network, trained via cross-modal distillation from a fixed, pre-trained image encoder, can learn to invert semantic class embeddings to generate high-fidelity, prototypical images that capture the essential visual features of a category.
proposals.method: We propose a method with two parallel decoders: a generator and a retrieval encoder. The generator is a diffusion model conditioned on the input class embedding (e.g., from CLIP or a supervised classifier). The retrieval encoder learns to map generated images back to embeddings. The system is trained end-to-end using a composite loss: a reconstruction loss between the original and retrieved embedding, a perceptual loss on the generated image, and an adversarial loss for realism. This mirrors a denoising distillation objective, where the fixed teacher encoder provides a stable target for the inversion process.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust framework for embedding inversion, enabling applications in explainable AI (visualizing model concepts) and efficient dataset summarization. It will also advance understanding of the semantics encoded in learned representation spaces by making them visually inspectable.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior inversion works focused on instance-level reconstruction, our method is designed for class-level prototype generation. It uniquely combines a dual-stream architecture with cross-modal distillation from a frozen teacher, creating a direct and stable pathway from embedding to image.

proposals.title: Incremental Class Learning via Cross-Modal Attention and Adaptive Embedding Distillation
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models are typically static; adding new visual concepts requires costly full retraining, which is inefficient and unsustainable for real-world applications. This problem is acute for large-scale vision-language models where new categories emerge constantly. An efficient method to incrementally learn novel class embeddings without catastrophic forgetting or retraining is a critical unsolved challenge in continual learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a frozen, pre-trained vision-language model can be extended to novel classes by learning a sparse set of new cross-modal attention pathways and distilling knowledge from a 3D-aware generative teacher model, enabling efficient incremental learning without performance degradation on base classes.
proposals.method: We will leverage a frozen CLIP-like model as a backbone. For each novel class, we will introduce lightweight, trainable adapter modules into the cross-attention layers of the text encoder. To generate rich, view-consistent features for novel classes with limited 2D data, we will use a pre-trained 3D-aware diffusion model (inspired by 3D denoisers as 2D teachers) to synthesize multiple views. These features will be distilled into the new adapter pathways via a contrastive alignment loss, regularized by a self-supervised consistency loss on base classes to prevent forgetting.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a parameter-efficient framework for lifelong learning in vision-language systems. It will demonstrate how 3D generative models can serve as robust teachers for 2D incremental learning, providing a new paradigm for data-efficient model updating. The resulting system will enable practical, on-the-fly expansion of visual recognition capabilities.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic use of 3D-aware generative distillation to create training signals for novel classes, combined with a sparse, attention-based adapter architecture for interference-free integration into a frozen model, moving beyond standard fine-tuning or replay-based continual learning.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Object-Centric Control via Self-Attention Routing and Denoising Diffusion
proposals.motivation: Current generative models for object-aware scene synthesis rely heavily on explicit, often costly, 'what' and 'where' supervision (e.g., bounding boxes, segmentation masks, or class labels). This limits their scalability and adaptability to new domains where such structured annotations are unavailable. A method that learns to discover, generate, and control objects from raw visual data alone would be a significant step toward more autonomous and general visual understanding systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained via a combination of denoising objectives and adaptive self-attention routing can learn to decompose scenes into controllable object representations without explicit conditioning signals, enabling both generation and manipulation based on high-level intent.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, an object-centric encoder will use iterative, adaptive self-attention blocks to route image patches into a set of latent slots, trained via a masked denoising objective inspired by 3D-to-2D distillation techniques. Second, a latent diffusion model will be trained on these slots, where guidance is provided not by explicit labels but by manipulating attention weights between slots and a learnable 'control token' that captures user intent (e.g., 'move', 'add', 'change'). Training will leverage large-scale unlabeled image datasets, with performance evaluated on zero-shot compositional generation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for unsupervised object-centric representation learning that bridges denoising pretraining and controllable generation. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating compositional reasoning in generative models without supervised conditioning, advancing toward more data-efficient and flexible scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of denoising-based pretraining for slot discovery with an attention-based control mechanism that inverts the standard paradigm, learning to interpret high-level instructions as manipulations of the model's internal attention pathways rather than relying on pre-defined conditioning inputs.

proposals.title: Generalizing Generative Adversarial What-Where Networks: A Scalability Study on Complex Multi-Object Scene Datasets
proposals.motivation: The GAWWN framework demonstrated impressive control over object location and pose in image generation, but its evaluation was largely confined to structured datasets of single objects like birds. For generative models to be useful in real-world applications such as content creation or data augmentation, they must handle complex scenes with diverse, interacting object categories. Testing GAWWN's limits on more challenging datasets is a critical step towards understanding the scalability of structured generative models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core GAWWN architecture, which combines a conditional GAN with spatial keypoint and mask supervision, can be successfully scaled to generate coherent images from complex, multi-object scene datasets, but will require enhancements in its attention and representation modules to manage increased scene complexity and category diversity.
proposals.method: We will adapt the GAWWN framework, retaining its core generator with a spatial transformer and discriminator with a matching-aware objective. We will train and evaluate on datasets like Visual Genome and COCO, which contain multiple objects per image with complex relationships. Key technical adaptations include: 1) augmenting the keypoint conditioning mechanism with a self-attention module to better model object-object interactions, and 2) employing a pre-trained, self-supervised visual encoder (e.g., based on MoCo or DINO) to derive richer, more disentangled feature representations for the 'what' vector, moving beyond simple class labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical benchmark for structured image generation on complex scenes, identifying failure modes and scalability bottlenecks of the GAWWN approach. It will contribute a modified architecture with improved conditioning mechanisms, setting a new state-of-the-art for controllable generation on multi-object datasets and offering insights for future model design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has applied conditional GANs to complex datasets, this study uniquely focuses on systematically testing and extending the specific 'what-where' pose-and-keypoint control paradigm of GAWWN beyond its original, narrow domain, which remains an open question in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Inverse Visual Instruction: Recovering Object-Centric 'What' and 'Where' Instructions from Single Images
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models excel at generating images from text but struggle with the inverse task: inferring the precise instructions that could have created a given scene. Solving this inverse problem is crucial for understanding model decision-making, enabling visual question answering about scene composition, and facilitating bi-directional editing between vision and language. It bridges the gap between generative and analytical visual understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained with a structured, object-centric instruction space can accurately decompose a complex image into a set of discrete 'what' (object class) and 'where' (spatial layout) instructions that are both human-interpretable and sufficient for a generative model to reconstruct the original scene.
proposals.method: We will leverage a two-stage framework. First, we will generate a synthetic dataset using a pretrained text-to-image diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion) and a structured instruction generator that produces tuples of (object, bounding box). Second, we will train a transformer-based encoder-decoder model. The vision encoder (e.g., ViT) will extract image features, which a decoder will map, via cross-attention, to a sequence of discrete instruction tokens representing object classes and normalized coordinates. Training will use a supervised loss against the ground-truth instruction sequences from the synthetic data.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new benchmark task for inverse graphics and structured scene understanding. It will provide a novel model capable of explicating image composition in a generative-action space, contributing to interpretability. The method could also serve as a perceptual backbone for instruction-following agents by inferring goals from visual observations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on captioning or dense segmentation, our approach uniquely formulates image understanding as the recovery of a generative instruction set, directly inverting the text-to-image process. This moves beyond descriptive captioning to actionable, compositional scene decomposition.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised Anomaly Detection via Cross-Modal Distributional Alignment
proposals.motivation: Current deep anomaly detection methods often rely on class-labeled data for training, which is expensive and limits scalability to diverse, unlabeled real-world datasets. The problem of detecting anomalies using only a general, unlabeled in-distribution set remains a significant challenge. This research aims to invert the need for class labels by leveraging recent advances in self-supervised representation learning and cross-modal understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained to align representations of in-distribution data across multiple self-supervised tasks will learn a robust, task-agnostic notion of normality, enabling the detection of anomalies as deviations from this aligned distribution without any class-specific supervision.
proposals.method: We will train a vision transformer backbone using a multi-task self-supervised objective combining masked image modeling and a novel cross-modal distillation task, inspired by Lee's prior work. The model will be trained exclusively on the unlabeled in-distribution set. For anomaly scoring, we will compute the Mahalanobis distance in the model's latent space and measure the reconstruction error from the masked autoencoder, fusing these scores. The cross-modal alignment, enforced via a student-teacher framework, will regularize the feature space to be compact for in-distribution samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, label-free framework for anomaly detection that is both scalable and performant. It will advance the understanding of how cross-modal and multi-task self-supervision can shape useful representations for out-of-distribution detection. The method will be benchmarked against supervised and semi-supervised baselines on standard datasets like CIFAR-10 and ImageNet.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly using cross-modal distributional alignment as a primary training objective for anomaly detection, rather than as an auxiliary task, to define normality without labels. This approach is distinct from prior generative or one-class methods that do not leverage multi-modal consistency constraints.

proposals.title: Adversarial Robustness of Mahalanobis OOD Detectors: A White-Box Attack and Defense Framework
proposals.motivation: Mahalanobis distance-based detectors are a prominent method for identifying out-of-distribution (OOD) and adversarial examples by leveraging feature representations from deep neural networks. However, their security is typically evaluated under a weak threat model where the adversary is unaware of the defense. In real-world deployment, an adaptive adversary with knowledge of the detection mechanism could craft attacks to bypass it, creating a critical vulnerability. This work addresses this gap by formally studying the robustness of these detectors against white-box adversaries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Mahalanobis-based OOD detection is highly vulnerable to white-box adversarial attacks that directly optimize inputs to minimize the detection score, and that a robust training objective incorporating this adversarial knowledge can significantly improve the detector's resilience.
proposals.method: We will develop a white-box attack that crafts adversarial examples by minimizing the Mahalanobis distance in the feature space of a pre-trained model, using gradient-based optimization with a penalty for maintaining classification confidence on a target class. We will then propose a novel adversarial training method for the detector, where the feature extractor (e.g., a supervised or self-supervised model) is fine-tuned using a min-max objective: the inner maximization generates attacks against the current detector, and the outer minimization updates the model's representations to maximize the Mahalanobis distance for these adversarial samples. This will draw on Lee's expertise in training robust representations and self-supervised methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive security audit of Mahalanobis-based detectors under a strong white-box threat model. It will yield a benchmark suite of adaptive attacks and a novel adversarial training defense, contributing a more rigorous framework for evaluating and improving the robustness of OOD detection systems in safety-critical applications.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial attacks on classifiers are well-studied, systematically attacking and defending a Mahalanobis-based *detection score* in a white-box setting is novel. Our proposed adversarial training directly targets the detector's decision boundary in the feature space, moving beyond standard adversarial training which only protects the classification output.

proposals.title: A Generalized Mahalanobis Framework for Out-of-Distribution Detection in Non-Softmax Classifiers
proposals.motivation: The Mahalanobis distance-based OOD detection method, built upon the penultimate layer features of a softmax classifier, is a seminal but limited framework. Many modern classifiers, such as contrastive learning models (e.g., SimCLR, SupCon), metric learning networks, and vision-language models (e.g., CLIP), do not rely on a final softmax layer, creating a gap in principled OOD detection for these widely-used architectures. A unified framework for these diverse classifier types is critically needed for robust real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generalized Mahalanobis framework, which estimates class-conditional distributions directly in the feature space and uses a task-appropriate scoring function, can provide effective and unified OOD detection for a broad spectrum of modern classifiers beyond softmax-based networks.
proposals.method: We will first formalize the feature statistics for different classifier types (contrastive, metric, multimodal) by computing per-class mean and covariance from their respective embedding spaces. We will then derive novel scoring functions: for contrastive models, we will use the distance to the nearest class prototype; for metric learners, a likelihood ratio based on learned distance metrics; and for models like CLIP, a text-guided density score. The framework will be evaluated on standard OOD benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10/100 vs. SVHN, Texture) using established metrics like AUROC and FPR95.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic extension of the Mahalanobis OOD detection principle to non-softmax classifiers, enabling robust uncertainty estimation for state-of-the-art models. It will deliver a practical, open-source toolkit for OOD detection applicable to contrastive, metric, and multimodal learning paradigms, significantly broadening the scope of reliable machine learning systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work adapts OOD methods to specific architectures in an ad-hoc manner, this proposal introduces a principled, unified generalization of a foundational distance-based method. The key novelty lies in deriving architecture-aware scoring rules from a common statistical foundation, moving beyond the softmax assumption that underpins all prior Mahalanobis-based detection.

proposals.title: Downscaled Feature Manifolds for Robust Few-Shot Learning via Self-Supervised Covariance Regularization
proposals.motivation: Extreme few-shot learning, where only 1-2 samples per class are available, critically fails because standard methods rely on reliable covariance estimation for feature adaptation or classifier calibration, which is impossible with such limited data. This severely limits the application of machine learning in domains like medical imaging or rare object recognition where labeled data is scarce. Existing meta-learning and feature adaptation methods degrade significantly in this regime, creating a key bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically downscaling the feature dimensionality of a pre-trained model, before few-shot adaptation, will create a denser and more stable feature manifold where within-class relationships can be reliably estimated even from a single sample via self-supervised constraints, thereby improving generalization.
proposals.method: We will leverage a large-scale pre-trained vision transformer (ViT) as a fixed feature extractor. A learnable linear down-projection layer will map features to a drastically reduced subspace (e.g., 16-64 dimensions). For adaptation, we will employ a self-supervised denoising objective inspired by pre-training methods, where we add noise to the downscaled features of the few support samples and train the projector to denoise them, effectively regularizing the manifold. Final classification will use a non-parametric cosine similarity-based classifier in this dense, regularized low-D space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, simple-to-implement method for extreme few-shot learning that bypasses the need for sample covariance estimation. It will contribute a theoretical and empirical analysis of feature density vs. dimensionality for few-shot robustness, and establish a new benchmark for 1-2 shot learning on standard datasets like miniImageNet and CUB.
proposals.novelty: While dimensionality reduction is classic, its application as a deliberate, learnable pre-adaptation step to *induce* reliable estimability for self-supervised regularization in the extreme few-shot regime is novel. This contrasts with prior work that either operates in high-dimensional spaces or uses reduction only after adaptation.

proposals.title: Temporal Self-Attention for Robust Video Sequence Analysis: Detecting OOD and Adversarial Frames via Cross-Modal Consistency
proposals.motivation: Video-based perception systems in autonomous agents and surveillance are vulnerable to out-of-distribution (OOD) scenes and adversarial attacks on sequential frames. Pre-2026, most OOD and adversarial detection methods are designed for static images, failing to exploit the rich temporal and multimodal consistency cues inherent in video. This gap creates critical safety and reliability risks for real-world sequential decision-making systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-supervised video representation model, trained to enforce spatio-temporal and cross-modal consistency, can learn robust temporal attention patterns that reliably highlight OOD or adversarially corrupted frames as violations of learned consistency rules.
proposals.method: We will extend principles from '3D Denoisers are Good 2D Teachers' and adaptive reward guidance. First, we will pretrain a 3D spatio-temporal transformer encoder via a denoising objective on large-scale video data. This model will serve as a teacher to distill robust sequential representations into a student detector network. The core detection mechanism will be an adaptive multimodal reward function, inspired by Honglak Lee's recent work, which scores each frame based on its consistency with (a) its temporal neighbors via self-attention maps and (b) a paired audio or text modality (using a NICE-style captioner). Significant deviations in this consistency 'reward' signal will flag anomalous frames.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, self-supervised framework for anomaly detection in video sequences that requires no OOD labels for training. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating temporal robustness in video models and offer a practical tool for enhancing the safety of sequential decision-making agents by pre-filtering corrupted inputs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing cross-modal distillation and adaptive reward mechanisms—previously used for generation and reinforcement learning—for the discriminative task of temporal anomaly detection, explicitly leveraging the failure of temporal self-attention under distribution shift as the primary detection signal.

proposals.title: Object-Centric World Models: Learning Compositional Dynamics via Structured Latent Attention
proposals.motivation: Current video prediction and world models often struggle with complex, long-horizon scenes involving multiple interacting objects, leading to blurry or inconsistent futures. This limits their utility for planning and reasoning in robotics and autonomous systems. Explicitly modeling objects as discrete, persistent entities is a promising but underexplored path toward more sample-efficient and compositional dynamics learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating object detection and modeling into a world model's latent space, using a structured attention mechanism, will enable more accurate and robust long-term prediction of object entry, departure, and interactions compared to monolithic pixel-level models.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, a pretrained object detector (e.g., DETR) provides object-centric slots for each frame. Second, a transformer-based dynamics model operates on these slots, using a novel Object-Persistent Cross-Attention (OPCA) module to track entities across time and model their interactions. The model will be trained via a combination of supervised reconstruction loss on future frames and a self-supervised contrastive loss on object representations to encourage consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architecture for object-aware world modeling that explicitly reasons about object permanence and interactions. It will provide new benchmarks for long-horizon video prediction in dynamic multi-object scenes and offer insights into how structured representations improve model generalization and data efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While object-centric learning exists, its integration into temporal dynamics models via a dedicated attention mechanism for persistent object tracking is novel. This moves beyond simply using object features as input to building an inductive bias for object persistence directly into the core dynamics learning process.

proposals.title: Adversarial Sharpening for Video Frame Prediction via Self-Supervised Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Video frame prediction models often produce blurry, unrealistic future frames due to the regression loss's tendency to average over multiple plausible futures. This limits their utility in critical applications like autonomous systems and video content creation, where perceptual realism and sharp detail are paramount. While Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) have revolutionized image synthesis, their integration into temporal prediction frameworks remains underexplored for generating sharp, temporally consistent sequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating an adversarial training objective, guided by self-supervised visual representations, into a temporal prediction model will enforce sharper and more perceptually realistic frame predictions without sacrificing long-term temporal coherence.
proposals.method: We will build upon a base video prediction architecture (e.g., a ConvLSTM or Transformer-based model) trained with a standard L1/L2 loss. We will introduce a conditional GAN framework where the generator is the predictor, and a spatio-temporal discriminator judges the realism of predicted sequences. Crucially, we will employ a self-supervised representation loss (e.g., using a pre-trained model like SimCLR or BYOL) as an adaptive multimodal reward signal for the generator, ensuring the predicted frames maintain high-level semantic consistency with the input context. The final objective will combine adversarial, representation, and pixel-wise reconstruction losses.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm for video prediction that systematically improves visual sharpness. It will provide new insights into using self-supervised representations as a stable, semantic guide for adversarial training in temporal domains. The resulting models will serve as stronger foundational tools for downstream vision-and-language tasks, such as those in the NICE challenge, where realistic visual dynamics are crucial.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of adversarial training with adaptive, self-supervised representation-based rewards for video prediction, moving beyond simple pixel-level GANs. This approach directly connects Dr. Lee's expertise in self-supervised representation learning and multimodal rewards to the classic problem of temporal modeling.

proposals.title: VideoMAE-Web: Masked Autoencoding for Scalable Self-Supervised Video Representation Learning from Noisy Web Data
proposals.motivation: The success of large-scale pretraining in NLP and image domains has not fully translated to video, where supervised datasets are expensive and small. Massive, unlabeled web video (e.g., YouTube) offers a vast, diverse source for learning rich spatiotemporal representations, but it is noisy and lacks reliable action labels. Developing methods to effectively learn from this data is crucial for scaling video understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reconstruction-based masked autoencoder (MAE) objective, combined with a cross-modal distillation signal from a strong, frozen image-text model, can learn robust and generalizable video representations from noisy web-scale data without any manual annotation.
proposals.method: We will extend the VideoMAE framework to web-scale data. We will use a high masking ratio (e.g., 90%) of spatiotemporal patches and train a ViT-based encoder-decoder to reconstruct the original pixels. To handle noise and inject semantic guidance, we will distill knowledge from a pre-trained, frozen CLIP model by aligning the latent features of the reconstructed video with CLIP's embeddings of the corresponding video clip, using a contrastive loss. This creates a joint objective of reconstruction and cross-modal feature alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, self-supervised method for video pretraining that reduces reliance on curated datasets. We will release a large-scale pretrained model and demonstrate its strong performance on downstream tasks like action recognition and video retrieval, establishing a new benchmark for web-scale video representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While VideoMAE exists, its application to noisy, uncurated web video with cross-modal distillation from image-text models for semantic regularization is novel. This combines the strengths of reconstruction-based self-supervision with the semantic grounding of vision-language models.

proposals.title: Sparse Reward Dynamics via Cross-Modal Temporal Attention and Self-Supervised Credit Assignment
proposals.motivation: Learning from extremely sparse and delayed rewards remains a fundamental bottleneck in reinforcement learning (RL), especially for long-horizon tasks like robotics or scientific discovery. Pre-2026 methods often rely on reward prediction or complex auxiliary objectives that can be inefficient or misaligned when the reward signal is non-Markovian and infrequent. This work challenges the prevailing assumption that an explicit reward prediction model is necessary for effective policy learning under such conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model can learn effective dynamics representations and policies from sparse, delayed rewards by using cross-modal distillation from a dense, self-supervised pretraining phase and a novel attention-based credit assignment mechanism, eliminating the need for explicit reward prediction.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage method. First, we pretrain a visual dynamics model using a dense, self-supervised temporal denoising objective on unlabeled interaction videos, inspired by 3D denoising diffusion methods. Second, we fine-tune the policy using only the sparse environmental rewards. Crucially, we replace standard reward prediction heads with an adaptive, cross-modal attention module that distills temporal representations from the pretrained model to guide credit assignment. This module, akin to a transformer decoder, attends to the pretrained dense features to weigh the importance of past states for the delayed reward, enabling effective backpropagation through time without reward prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new framework for sparse-reward RL that decouples dynamics learning from reward prediction, potentially improving sample efficiency and final performance in long-horizon tasks. It will also provide insights into how self-supervised visual pretraining and attention mechanisms can be leveraged for temporal credit assignment, advancing the field of representation learning for control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly removing the reward prediction model—a standard component in many model-based and model-free RL algorithms—and replacing it with a cross-modal attention mechanism that repurposes a self-supervised dynamics model for credit assignment, a non-obvious synergy given prior work.

ideas.title: Scale up with web-scale image-text pairs using a 'noisy channel' distillation filter
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from bioinformatics, where coherent genomes are assembled from millions of noisy, short DNA sequence reads. The analogous problem is training a joint embedding model on millions of loosely aligned (noisy) web images and captions. The solution involves creating a pipeline analogous to overlap-layout-consensus and error correction. Before full-scale training, the web-scale data is processed through a filtering stage that uses weak consistency heuristics—such as semantic similarity between caption and generated image tags, confidence scores from pre-trained object detectors, and entropy measures from a lightweight cross-attention probe—to score and weight the reliability of each image-text pair. This assembles a distilled, higher-alignment training corpus from the noisy web data, enabling more effective scaling of the joint embedding model by focusing compute on the most informative signals.

ideas.title: Joint VAE-Discriminative Training via a Symbiotic Feedback Loop
ideas.description: This idea proposes integrating a generative VAE with a strong discriminative model not merely through multi-task learning, but by engineering a symbiotic feedback loop inspired by cross-feeding in synthetic microbial communities. The analogous solution from systems biology involves designing explicit metabolic dependencies to stabilize a consortium. Adapted to machine learning, the system would couple the models by having the discriminative classifier's loss gradients influence the VAE's latent prior or reconstruction penalty, steering it to learn features salient for the discriminative task. Concurrently, the VAE's generative output provides augmented or adversarially challenging examples to train a more robust classifier. This creates a stable, joint training regime where each model's 'output' feeds and improves the other, leading to superior shared feature representations and overall system performance.

ideas.description: This approach introduces a structured fusion architecture with separate encoders for attribute vectors and raw text. A central cross-modal gating mechanism dynamically balances the contribution of each modality per input instance, preventing dominance. An auxiliary consistency loss is applied to align the semantic representations extracted from each modality, ensuring they complement rather than contradict each other. This results in a more robust model that generalizes better by learning truly complementary signals from structured and unstructured side information.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Graphical Attribute Completion for Coherent Conditional Generation
ideas.description: This research direction develops a conditional generative framework where a probabilistic graphical model (PGM), such as a Bayesian network or a factor graph, explicitly encodes the joint distribution and causal/temporal relationships between attributes. The generative model, typically a deep neural network, is conditioned not on raw, potentially noisy attributes, but on a posterior distribution over the complete attribute space inferred by the PGM from the partial, uncertain, or contradictory input. This allows the system to generate data by sampling from the most probable coherent interpretations of the user's intent, effectively performing joint inference and generation in a single, principled pipeline. The approach moves beyond simple attribute imputation by using the structured dependencies within the PGM to guide the generative process towards globally consistent and semantically plausible outputs.

ideas.title: Inverse Seismic Imaging: Generate Fine-Grained Text from Images via Constrained Decoding in a Joint Space
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the inverse problem in seismic imaging, where subsurface properties are inferred from seismic data using a physics-constrained model space. The analogous problem in vision-language is generating fine-grained text from a novel image. The adaptation involves using the learned joint image-text embedding space as the constrained model. The solution is to perform 'inverse generation': starting from the image's point in the joint space, we iteratively decode a textual description. This decoding is constrained by the forward compatibility of the joint space (i.e., the generated text should map back near the original image embedding), ensuring the output is a detailed, accurate description that is grounded in the visual data, much like how seismic inversion ensures geological models match the recorded waveforms.

ideas.title: Stability-Preserved Joint Training via Adaptive Gradient Equilibrium
ideas.description: This redesigned system integrates a VAE with a discriminative model but enforces a stable equilibrium between their learning objectives. It uses projection-based gradient surgery to align the updates from the two components, minimizing conflict. A stability monitor continuously evaluates the VAE's reconstruction fidelity and latent space regularity, triggering adaptive loss re-weighting if the generative quality degrades. This ensures robust joint feature learning without sacrificing the integrity of the generative model.

ideas.title: Web-Scale Ambiguity-Aware Retrieval and Completion
ideas.description: This research direction develops a large-scale, web-level image retrieval system that is robust to imperfect queries. It leverages efficient indexing of learned embeddings to search billions of images, but crucially, its core model is trained to handle partial, noisy, or contradictory attribute sets in the query text. The system's novel capability is to perform 'retrieval with completion,' where it can infer the most probable missing attributes from the data distribution and the provided context, returning results that satisfy both the explicit and the plausibly completed constraints. This enables intuitive, human-like search where users do not need perfectly specified prompts, moving beyond strict attribute matching to semantic understanding and hypothesis generation at scale.

ideas.title: Ontology-Guided Generative Modeling for Structurally Diverse Object Categories
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a layered generative model that is conditioned on and guided by structured ontologies (e.g., ConceptNet, WordNet, or domain-specific knowledge graphs) when extending to new object categories. Instead of treating categories as flat labels, the model uses the ontological relationships—such as part-of, is-a, and has-property—to structure its latent space, explicitly separating part-level, attribute-level, and categorical semantics. This allows for the coherent generation of visually plausible and semantically consistent instances for categories with complex internal structure (e.g., vehicles, furniture, or articulated animals), which are difficult for models trained only on raw text and images. The key innovation is that the ontology provides the necessary compositional blueprint for the model to generalize systematically across a wide, structurally diverse range of categories, enabling controllable generation and interpolation based on semantic constraints.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Adaptive Metric Learning with Meta-Optimization
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond a static metric by learning to generate or select an appropriate distance function conditioned on the data context or a specific task goal. A meta-learning framework trains the system on a distribution of related tasks or semantic scales, teaching the model to warp the embedding space geometry to optimize for different downstream objectives, such as fine-grained discrimination versus coarse-grained categorization. This results in a single, flexible embedding model that can produce semantically meaningful similarity judgments aligned with variable human or task-defined notions of relevance, significantly improving robustness and generalization.

ideas.title: Joint Structure Learning and Inference for Robust Attribute Dependency Modeling
ideas.description: This system jointly learns the probabilistic graphical model's structure and its parameters from observational data, eliminating the need for a potentially incorrect manual specification. It employs regularization and hypothesis testing to infer a sparse, interpretable dependency network among attributes while quantifying uncertainty over the learned relationships. The final model performs robust inference by aggregating predictions across an ensemble of high-probability graph structures, ensuring accuracy even when the true dependencies are complex or partially obscured. This approach adapts the model to the data's inherent structure rather than forcing the data to conform to a pre-conceived dependency graph.

ideas.title: Semantic Knowledge Fusion via Constraint-Based Graph Embedding
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to improve embedding quality by fusing raw text descriptions with structured knowledge bases, drawing an analogy from computational biology's success in protein structure prediction. In that field, systems like AlphaFold2 integrate raw sequence data with structured physical and evolutionary constraints using deep learning to satisfy multiple objective constraints simultaneously. Adapted to NLP, the idea is to design a constraint-based graph embedding model where a neural architecture learns a joint representation by optimizing an objective that combines standard text reconstruction loss with explicit consistency constraints derived from an external knowledge base or ontology (e.g., enforcing that embeddings for 'dog' and 'animal' respect the 'is-a' hierarchy, or that relation triples from a knowledge graph form consistent geometric transformations). This approach forces the final embeddings to be informed by both the distributional semantics of large text corpora and the explicit, structured semantic knowledge, potentially leading to more accurate, robust, and logically consistent representations.

Generation then proceeds conditionally on this reconciled, coherent latent representation, ensuring outputs are both faithful to the plausible intent behind the inputs and semantically consistent.

ideas.title: Scene Representation via Continuous Neural Fields
ideas.description: This idea challenges the explicit layered image model by drawing an analogy from Computational Fluid Dynamics. In CFD, fluids are modeled not as discrete layers but as continuous fields governed by partial differential equations. Adapting this, we propose representing a visual scene not with explicit foreground/background layers, but as a holistic, continuous neural field. A neural network (like a multi-layer perceptron) would be trained to map any spatial coordinate (x, y, t) to a full set of scene attributes (RGB color, density, feature embedding). This implicit representation allows for a fluid and unified scene model where object separation, transparency, and depth are emergent properties of the learned field, enabling more flexible scene editing, view synthesis, and reasoning about occlusions without rigid layer assumptions.

ideas.title: Apply auditory scene analysis attention for visual composition
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Computational Auditory Scene Analysis (CASA), where attention mechanisms segregate and softly recombine concurrent sound streams (like voice and noise) based on perceptual cues, to the visual domain. The analogous problem in machine learning is the soft composition of foreground and background visual features. The adapted research idea is to design a visual attention module inspired by auditory stream segregation. This module would first separate feature streams corresponding to foreground objects and background context using learned visual grouping principles, then use a spatial attention mechanism, similar to those in machine translation but guided by CASA's fusion strategy, to dynamically and optimally recombine these streams. This approach aims to provide a more structured and perceptually grounded method for feature composition than standard attention, potentially improving model performance on tasks requiring nuanced scene understanding.

ideas.title: Category-Agnostic Layered Scene Generation with Explicit Geometric Control
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel layered generative model that integrates Spatial Transformer Networks (STNs) at its core to handle a wide array of object categories. The model explicitly learns to decompose a scene into foreground object layers and a background, where each foreground layer is equipped with an STN to predict and apply its specific geometric transformation (e.g., rotation, scaling, non-rigid deformation). This architectural synergy allows the model to generate coherent scenes from novel object categories by learning a general principle of geometric layer separation, rather than category-specific patterns. Consequently, it enables fine-grained control over the pose, size, and placement of individual objects within a generated composite scene, a capability beyond standard category expansion or spatial modeling alone.

ideas.title: Architecture-Aware Indexing for Scalable, Compositional Cross-Modal Retrieval
ideas.description: This research investigates a tight co-design of advanced neural language architectures (like hierarchical attention or memory-augmented networks) and the large-scale indexing system required for web-scale retrieval. The core synergy is that the indexing mechanism (e.g., a learned hierarchical or graph-based index) is not generic but is explicitly structured to mirror and exploit the compositional and relational representations learned by the sophisticated text encoder. This allows the system to efficiently handle complex, long-tail queries involving compositionality, negation, or relational reasoning by traversing the index in a semantically-aware manner, moving beyond simple nearest-neighbor search. Consequently, the retrieval system gains the ability to answer nuanced queries at scale, a capability neither improved architectures alone nor brute-force scaling of simple embeddings could achieve.

ideas.title: Bayesian Imputation Networks for Conditional Generation with Uncertain Attributes
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from Ecology and Species Distribution Modeling, where Bayesian hierarchical models handle incomplete, noisy, and contradictory field data to predict species distributions. The analogous solution treats missing or uncertain variables as latent parameters, integrating all evidence to generate probabilistic predictions. Adapted to conditional generation, the idea proposes Bayesian Imputation Networks that model incomplete, uncertain, or contradictory attributes as latent variables with learned priors. The system would sample from the posterior distribution of attributes given the noisy input, then condition a generative model on these samples. This enables generation that reflects the inherent uncertainty, producing a distribution of plausible outputs consistent with the partial or conflicting specifications, rather than a single deterministic result.

ideas.title: Adaptive Spectral Unmixing for Scene Disentanglement
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of spectral unmixing from remote sensing to improve background modeling and disentanglement in computer vision. In hyperspectral imaging, linear mixing models and algorithms like NMF disentangle the combined spectral signal of multiple materials within a single pixel. Adapted to this research context, we propose treating the latent feature representation of a scene as a mixture. The method would involve learning a dictionary of fundamental background and foreground feature patterns ('endmembers'). For a novel, cluttered image, an adaptive unmixing algorithm would then decompose its feature activations into a sparse linear combination of these patterns. This provides an explicit, interpretable separation of the foreground object's feature contribution from the complex, overlapping contributions of various background elements, directly addressing the core disentanglement challenge.

ideas.title: Knowledge Distillation via Progressive Resolution Reduction for Mobile Retrieval
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from medical imaging—progressive resolution reduction for diagnostic systems in resource-constrained clinics—to create a lightweight model for real-time text retrieval on mobile devices. The analogous medical solution uses a fast, low-resolution screening model to identify critical regions before applying more computationally intensive high-resolution analysis only where needed. Adapted to this context, the idea involves designing a multi-stage retrieval pipeline where a tiny 'screening' model (operating on compressed semantic representations or hashed features) performs initial coarse retrieval from a large document corpus. Only the top candidates from this first pass are then processed by a more capable, but still efficient, 'refinement' model for precise re-ranking. This hierarchical, progressive approach ensures that the majority of computational work is handled by the smallest model possible, enabling real-time performance on mobile hardware while maintaining retrieval accuracy through targeted use of more sophisticated models.

ideas.title: Contrastive Consensus Learning with Dynamic Knowledge Integration for Robust Embeddings
ideas.description: This system dynamically integrates multiple, potentially conflicting structured knowledge sources (ontologies, KBs, schemas) with raw text by modeling their assertions probabilistically. Instead of enforcing a rigid alignment, it learns embeddings through a contrastive objective that pulls text representations toward a consensus derived from the knowledge sources while pushing away from contradictory or unsupported assertions. A gating mechanism, trained to detect novelty and conflict, adaptively weights the influence of the structured consensus versus the raw text semantics, ensuring robustness to incomplete or erroneous knowledge. This results in embeddings that are informed by structured knowledge where it is reliable and consistent, but resilient to its gaps and biases.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding's Hierarchical Attention for Multi-Scale Text Representation
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from the breakthrough in protein structure prediction by AlphaFold2. In that field, the core problem was integrating disparate, multi-scale biological signals (residue pairs, sequences, evolutionary data) into a coherent 3D structure. The analogous solution was the Evoformer's sophisticated attention mechanism, which processes information hierarchically and allows different 'representations' (e.g., for pairs and for single residues) to iteratively communicate. Adapted to neural language modeling, the research would investigate a novel architecture that employs separate but interconnected attention modules dedicated to different linguistic scales (morphological, syntactic, discourse). These modules would exchange information through a dedicated communication mechanism, akin to the pair-to-residue and residue-to-pair attention in the Evoformer. The goal is to produce text representations in a joint embedding space that more explicitly and effectively captures the hierarchical structure of language, leading to better performance on tasks requiring deep semantic and structural understanding.

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Hierarchical Synthesis for High-Resolution Attribute-Conditioned Image Generation
ideas.description: This research adapts the multi-scale hierarchical modeling principle from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), specifically Large Eddy Simulation (LES), to the problem of scaling attribute-conditioned image generation to high resolutions. In LES, large-scale turbulent structures are simulated directly while smaller, more universal scales are efficiently modeled. Analogously, we propose a cascade of layered VAEs for image synthesis: a primary VAE generates a low-resolution semantic layout from input attributes. Subsequent, specialized VAE modules then operate on progressively higher resolutions, each tasked with synthesizing the image details pertinent to a specific scale or frequency band (e.g., mid-scale structures, fine textures). Each finer-scale VAE is conditioned on both the original attributes and the upscaled output of the previous coarser stage, ensuring coherence. This decomposes the monolithic high-resolution generation task into a hierarchy of simpler, scale-specific problems, improving training stability, computational efficiency, and the fidelity of photorealistic detail compared to direct single-model approaches.

ideas.title: Adaptive Visual Attention via Cognitive Load Balancing
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of using visual attention mechanisms by drawing an analogy from adaptive learning systems in HCI and Educational Psychology. The analogous solution uses real-time monitoring (eye-tracking) and performance feedback to dynamically balance a learner's cognitive load, guiding attention to critical instructional elements. Adapted to computer vision, this translates to an adaptive visual attention mechanism for fine-grained image-text tasks. The model would not just apply a pre-trained attention map but would implement a meta-control loop: it evaluates its own attention distribution and alignment performance during processing, then dynamically adjusts its subsequent focus. This allows the model to learn to allocate more 'computational attention' to challenging or semantically dense image regions mentioned in the text, effectively balancing its 'cognitive load' between visual search and linguistic grounding for improved fine-grained understanding.

ideas.title: Grounded Hybrid Representations: Discrete Semantic Scaffolds for Continuous and Textual Features
ideas.description: This approach constructs visual representations by first parsing an input into a discrete, symbolic graph defined by a domain ontology (e.g., parts, materials, spatial relations). This graph acts as a semantic scaffold, ensuring explicit compositional structure. A neural module then generates continuous feature vectors and free-form text descriptions that are explicitly conditioned on and regularized by this scaffold, grounding them in shared semantics. This enables precise, structure-aware editing via the discrete graph while leveraging the expressivity of continuous features and text for generation and fine-grained similarity.

ideas.title: Adapting Genomic Sequence Alignment for Web-Scale Embedding Indexing
ideas.description: The challenge of scaling a retrieval system to web-scale datasets mirrors the problem in computational genomics of aligning a query DNA sequence against a massive reference database. In genomics, solutions like MinHash and locality-sensitive hashing are used to create approximate, lossy indexes ('sketches') that enable scalable search. This analogy can be adapted for large-scale text-based image retrieval. The adapted research idea is to develop a novel indexing framework that generates compact, hash-based sketches from the learned multimodal embeddings. By applying these bioinformatics-inspired approximate indexing techniques, the system can perform an initial, highly efficient broad-scale search across billions of entries. The resulting small candidate set is then passed to a more computationally expensive, precise re-ranking stage using the original high-dimensional embeddings, achieving a practical balance between retrieval accuracy and scalability for web-sized datasets.

ideas.title: Apply Phylogenetic Distance Constraints from Bioinformatics to Metric Learning
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution principles from bioinformatics, specifically phylogenetic tree construction, to improve metric learning. In phylogenetics, algorithms enforce known hierarchical distance constraints (e.g., species within a genus are closer than species in different families) to build an evolutionary embedding (the tree). The adapted research idea is to use these hierarchical relational constraints as a structured, prior-informed form of triplet or contrastive loss. By defining 'clades' or groups based on a known taxonomy or ontology (e.g., for objects: 'dog' -> 'retriever' -> 'golden retriever'), the loss function would explicitly minimize distances within fine-grained groups and enforce proportional separation between groups at different hierarchical levels. This creates a joint embedding space that not only separates classes but also reflects the inherent semantic structure of the data, leading to more interpretable and robust representations.

ideas.title: Compositional Hierarchical Deformation Networks for Complex Object Geometry
ideas.description: This system addresses the rigidity of standard STNs by decomposing foreground object geometry into semantically meaningful parts via an auxiliary segmentation head. A cascade of local spatial transformers, each conditioned on a specific part mask, then estimates dense, non-parametric displacement fields for their respective regions. A final fusion network composites these local deformation fields, regularized by a smoothness constraint across part boundaries, to produce a coherent, complex transformation of the entire object. This approach enables the explicit modeling of articulated, non-rigid, and highly variable object geometries beyond global parametric transforms.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Network Models for Attribute Dependency Graphs
ideas.description: This research adapts solution strategies from evolutionary biology's phylogenetic network modeling to the problem of encoding attribute dependencies in machine learning. In phylogenetics, when simple evolutionary trees cannot explain observed genetic data due to events like hybridization or horizontal gene transfer, researchers employ probabilistic graphical models structured as networks (e.g., Bayesian phylogenetic networks, neighbor-nets) to represent reticulate relationships and infer ancestral states. The adapted idea applies this paradigm to attribute relationship modeling: instead of forcing dependencies into a tree (like a Bayesian network tree), construct an attribute dependency network that can capture multi-parent influences, horizontal correlations between attribute clusters, and latent 'ancestral' attributes. This allows for more expressive modeling of complex, real-world attribute interdependencies—such as in healthcare data where a symptom may arise from multiple disease pathways, or in consumer data where preferences blend across categories—improving feature representation, imputation, and causal inference.

ideas.title: Spatial Transformer Fields for Fluid Scene Representation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes Spatial Transformer Fields, a network that parameterizes a dense, continuous field of local affine or thin-plate spline transformations over the image coordinate space. Instead of isolating and transforming discrete foreground layers, this field is applied holistically, warping the feature map or output image in a spatially-fluid manner. The model learns to apply significant transformations in regions corresponding to object semantics while applying near-identity transformations in background areas, all within a single, unified deformation field. This achieves the geometric control of Spatial Transformer Networks while adhering to the relaxed, non-layered scene representation, enabling novel view synthesis and image-based rendering where scene elements deform and interact continuously.

ideas.title: Extend Layered Generative Model via Taxonomic Hierarchy and Shared Primitives
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from evolutionary biology and comparative anatomy to the problem of extending a layered generative model to more object categories. In comparative anatomy, diverse species are understood through homologous structures—variations of shared anatomical blueprints organized within a taxonomic hierarchy. The analogous solution is to systematically compare organisms by mapping these variations onto a tree of life. Adapted to the generative model context, the idea is to define a foundational set of 'generative primitives' or reusable latent structure templates (e.g., for symmetry, part decomposition, topological connectivity) that are shared across object categories. The model is then extended to new categories not from scratch, but by learning how to recombine, parameterize, and perhaps inhibit these primitives in category-specific ways. This process can be guided by a pre-defined or learned lightweight semantic or structural taxonomy of object categories, ensuring a principled and data-efficient expansion pathway beyond the initial domains of faces and birds.

ideas.title: Adapting Chemical Functional Group Theory for Structured Attribute Representations
ideas.description: This idea adapts the concept of functional groups from organic chemistry to create richer, structured attribute representations. In chemistry, functional groups are relational substructures that predict behavior; here, we propose defining 'attribute functional groups' as reusable, graph-based templates encoding features, relations, and constraints (e.g., for 'social hierarchy', a template might include relations like 'reports_to', 'has_authority_over', and associated constraints). This moves beyond simple labels, allowing AI models to reason about how complex attributes interact, combine, and influence an object's properties or behavior, enabling more nuanced understanding and prediction.

ideas.title: Use Non-Negative Matrix Factorization for Latent Theme Discovery in Text
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry and spectral analysis to the problem of discovering latent visual themes in text. In spectral mixture analysis, Non-Negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) is used to unmix observed spectra into the latent spectral signatures of pure components and their concentrations, yielding interpretable, parts-based representations. By applying NMF to a document-term matrix constructed from text descriptions, we can decompose it into a document-theme matrix and a theme-term matrix. This provides a probabilistic, additive, and highly interpretable topic model where each discovered visual theme is represented as a non-negative combination of words, directly revealing the constituent semantic features that define the latent theme.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Adaptive Cascade for Robust On-Device Retrieval
ideas.description: This system introduces a dynamic, multi-model framework for robust text retrieval on mobile and embedded devices. Instead of a single downsized model, it maintains a cascade of specialized models with varying size-speed-accuracy trade-offs. A lightweight runtime controller continuously monitors device state and query intent, dynamically routing requests to the optimal model and execution pathway to meet real-time latency and energy constraints. This ensures consistent, reliable performance across the highly variable conditions of mobile use, from offline low-power mode to connected high-performance scenarios.

ideas.title: Adaptive Geometric Warping via Biomechanical Deformation Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from computational biomechanics, where soft tissue deformation is accurately modeled using finite element methods with hyperelastic material models (e.g., Neo-Hookean) that predict non-linear, physics-based deformations. The analogous solution is adapted by constraining a spatial transformer network to learn deformation parameters interpretable as physical forces or material properties acting on a simulated elastic mesh representation of the foreground object. This replaces generic transformation modules with a differentiable physics-based deformation layer, injecting physically plausible priors and improving generalization for modeling realistic geometric transformations of objects under various conditions, such as viewpoint changes, articulation, or soft deformation.
